decisions.emotion_targeted: Confidence that the determinism axiom is being honored precisely, not applied so broadly that it blocks legitimate, harmless diagnostic tooling.
decisions.misconception_created: A future reader grepping for Date.now and finding hits in tools/profiler/ might think the axiom was violated; the validation criterion (a scan confirming src/core|systems|components|entities|schema|eval|gen stay clean of wall-clock reads) is the actual guarantee, not a blanket repo-wide ban.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Banning wall-clock reads everywhere: makes REQ-132 unbuildable before P9/P11, contradicting its P8,P11 phase column which expects a real P8 share now. Threading a fake/simulated clock through profiling instead: adds indirection with no benefit since profiler output never touches gameplay state or replay hashes in the first place.

decisions.title: P8 death-heatmap coordinates are replay-derived, never live-position-logged
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-133 wants death-coordinate heatmaps. A naive reading logs the player's live world position continuously during play. But every level in this project is already fully reconstructible from (level, seed, InputFrame tape) via the P1-proven deterministic replay guarantee — a second, parallel live-position-logging path would duplicate that guarantee with an unverified new mechanism, and would be actual new capture-surface the project does not otherwise need.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Telemetry capture (S8.5) records only the minimal replayable unit: {levelId, seed, tape: ReplayTape, outcome} — exactly the shape TapeAnalyzer's analyzeTape already expects as an ArchetypeRun (dm-0044's data-substitution contract, satisfied with zero new capture surface). Death coordinates (S8.6) are recovered by deterministically replaying that capture through the existing Engine and sampling the player position at the tick the defeated transition fires. This is also literally REQ-133's "input recording for failure analysis" clause — the same captured tape, filtered/indexed by outcome.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Confidence that telemetry is exactly as trustworthy as every other verified subsystem here — derived from the same replay guarantee, not a second unverified data path.
decisions.misconception_created: A reader might expect a dedicated "position logger" module; there isn't one — death coordinates are a query against replay, not a capture-time concern.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Continuous live position logging: doubles the capture surface, is unverified against the replay guarantee, and stores far more data than the heatmap actually needs. Sampling position only at scripted checkpoints during live play: still a second capture path; replay-derived sampling needs none.

decisions.title: P8 telemetry reuses P6 CampaignDirector for difficulty-spike detection instead of building a second algorithm
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-133 names "statistical difficulty spikes" as a telemetry deliverable. P6's own exit condition (docs/verification/P6.md) is literally "flags a synthetic difficulty spike": CampaignDirector.processCampaign + ChapterHealth already derive CampaignAlerts, including a difficulty-spike class, from BehaviorSignals-bearing ChapterRecords. Writing a second spike-detection algorithm in tools/telemetry/ would duplicate verified P6 logic and create two sources of truth for the same signal.
decisions.problem_it_solves: P8's sole remaining obligation for the spike clause is data supply: feed live-captured records through the existing analyzeTape (dm-0044) to derive BehaviorSignals, assemble a ChapterRecord, and run the existing, unmodified processCampaign (P6) — never build new detection logic. This is precisely the "data substitution, not a structural change" contract dm-0044 pre-authorized, and precisely why REQ-032's P6 share could already flip VERIFIED at P6 close while its P8 share remained real, unbuilt engineering.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Trust that one signal has exactly one algorithm computing it, regardless of which phase supplied the data feeding that algorithm.
decisions.misconception_created: Someone reading "P8 delivers telemetry-driven difficulty spikes" might expect new spike math in tools/; the validation criterion at S8.6 explicitly requires reuse (a test asserting zero diff against P6 fixtures' alert shape), not reinvention.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: A second spike-detection algorithm tuned for live telemetry: duplicates P6's already-verified ChapterHealth/CampaignAlert logic, risking the two implementations drifting apart over time. Modifying campaign/ to add a "live" mode: violates dm-0047's campaign/ isolation rule (P8 does not modify src/eval/campaign/).

decisions.title: P8 debug variable manipulation commits through StateManager, never mutates a live snapshot in place
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-131's "runtime inspection controls... manipulate variables" reads like a debugger backdoor that directly edits fields on a running WorldState snapshot — exactly the "mutate active data buffers in place" the core immutability invariant (dm-0004/dm-0043) forbids.
decisions.problem_it_solves: tools/debug/Inspector.ts's setVariable expresses a debug edit as an explicit state patch, applied the same way any system's output is applied — through StateManager.commit() — never a direct field write on a live snapshot. This keeps debug tooling inside the same single-mutation-point discipline every system already honors, at zero extra engineering cost since StateManager was built generically from S1.6 onward. The existing freeze-on-commit test pattern extends to catch a regression here.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Confidence that even the most invasive-sounding tool in the project (a live variable editor) cannot quietly break the immutability guarantee every other system depends on.
decisions.misconception_created: A reader might assume "variable manipulation" means unrestricted field access; it means committing a validated state patch through the one sanctioned mutation point, same as a physics or lifecycle system tick.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: A direct-mutation debug editor for convenience/speed: violates dm-0004/dm-0043 outright and risks corrupting `previous`-snapshot invariants relied on elsewhere (e.g. render interpolation, once P9 exists). A separate "debug state" shadow copy reconciled later: adds a second source of truth exactly where StateManager already provides one canonical commit point.

decisions.title: S8.1 clarifies dm-0066: CANONICAL_PIPELINE (AgentHarness) is a named tools/ public seam into src/eval/
decisions.why_it_exists: dm-0066 pre-registered a whitelist of public src/eval/ entry points tools/ may consume (evaluateLevel/judgeLevel, the P4 audits, analyzeTape, processCampaign, manufactureLevel, discoverOpportunities) before S8.1 code was written. Implementing the S8.1 live-playtest driver (tools/level_editor/Playtest.ts) surfaced a real omission: assembling the Engine at all requires CANONICAL_PIPELINE, the normative system order exported from src/eval/AgentHarness.ts -- CLAUDE.md itself calls it "the only sanctioned engine assembly order". That constant was not on the pre-registered list.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Rather than duplicating CANONICAL_PIPELINE as a second array inside tools/ (a single-source-of-truth violation risking drift between the eval-side and tools-side simulation if a system is ever added or reordered), Playtest.ts imports CANONICAL_PIPELINE directly from src/eval/AgentHarness.ts. This decision formally adds AgentHarness (for CANONICAL_PIPELINE only) to the dm-0066 whitelist, enforced by test/unit/ToolsIsolation.test.ts alongside the pre-existing campaign/ (analyzeTape/processCampaign) allowance.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Confidence that the isolation rule reflects what the code actually needs, not a first-draft guess frozen before any implementation existed.
decisions.misconception_created: A reader diffing dm-0066 against the ToolsIsolation scan might think the scan is under-enforcing; this entry records why AgentHarness is allowed alongside campaign/, not omitted by oversight.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Re-deriving/duplicating the six-system pipeline array inside tools/level_editor/Playtest.ts: creates a second source of truth for the single most load-bearing assembly order in the project, risking silent divergence between eval-side and tools-side simulation. Importing the six systems individually and re-freezing an equivalent array: identical duplication risk with extra code.

decisions.title: REQ-132 asset-delivery-speed clause is a P9 share; backlog phase column P8,P11 -> P8,P9,P11
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-132 enumerates four profiling targets: frame rate, memory allocations, scene load times, AND asset delivery speeds. The first three are measurable now against the existing engine (S8.4 delivers them: timeSection for frame/section timing, countAllocations for allocations, sceneLoadTiming for scene load). But asset delivery speed presupposes an asset pipeline -- textures, audio, sprite atlases -- none of which exists before P9 (Rendering, Audio & Visual Grammar). This is the SAME class of finding as dm-0065 (REQ-130/131 presuppose a rendering surface P9 builds): a requirement whose clauses span the P8/P9 boundary.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Prevents S8.4/S8.7 from silently claiming full REQ-132 coverage when one of its four named clauses is genuinely unbuildable in P8. The backlog phase column is amended P8,P11 -> P8,P9,P11 (exact-string edit, diff-verified): P8 delivers frame/allocation/scene-load profiling, P9 adds asset-delivery-speed profiling once an asset pipeline exists, P11 owns the release-audit profiling share. REQ-132 stays IN_PROGRESS through all three. Same treatment, same precedent (dm-0008 multi-phase discipline) as REQ-150 at P3 and REQ-130/131 at S8.1/S8.2.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Honesty in the four-state ledger -- a requirement is only VERIFIED when every clause it names is genuinely built, not when the phase that owned the buildable clauses closes.
decisions.misconception_created: A reader might think S8.4 under-delivered REQ-132; it delivered every clause that is buildable against the pre-P9 engine, and the phase column now documents exactly which clause waits for P9.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Building a stub asset-delivery-speed profiler in P8: there are no assets to measure delivery of -- it would measure nothing, a placeholder (forbidden). Claiming full REQ-132 at S8.7 despite the missing clause: falsifies the ledger. Leaving the phase column P8,P11: would let REQ-132 wrongly appear fully P8-owned-plus-release, hiding the P9 dependency.

decisions.title: TelemetryProfile is a fourth versioned calibration schema (heatmap bin size); resolves P8 plan open question 1
decisions.why_it_exists: S8.6 death-heatmap binning needs a bin-size constant. The data-driven axiom forbids a numeric literal in telemetry logic. P8 plan open question 1 asked whether this lives in a new TelemetryProfile or an extension of CampaignProfile.
decisions.problem_it_solves: A new tools/telemetry/TelemetryProfile.ts -- a fourth versioned, strict-parsed calibration record (telemetryProfileSchemaVersion, DEFAULT_TELEMETRY_PROFILE frozen, path-qualified parse, unknown-key/bad-version/non-positive rejection) alongside ScoringProfile/CampaignProfile/GenProfile. Following dm-0057 exactly: telemetry calibration is a P8-owned concern (heatmap bin size now; P9/P11 may add asset-timing or spike-sensitivity constants), so coupling it to the verified CampaignProfile would force a version bump on a document that did not change meaning. binWorldSize default 1 (one world unit); deriveDeathHeatmap bins by floor(pos/binWorldSize). Zero literals in DeathHeatmap.ts.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Consistency -- a fourth calibration concern gets a fourth schema, the same separation-of-concerns discipline the first three got, rather than an ad hoc constant or a coupling that muddies CampaignProfile.
decisions.misconception_created: A full versioned strict-parsed schema for one number may look heavy; it is the established project pattern (GenProfile started nearly as small at S7.1 and grew per slice), and it gives P9/P11 telemetry-calibration additions a home without a later refactor.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Extending CampaignProfile: couples P8 telemetry calibration to a verified P6 schema, forcing version bumps on an unchanged document (exactly what dm-0057 rejected for GenProfile). A bare constant in DeathHeatmap.ts: violates the data-driven axiom (no numeric literals in logic). A plain unversioned options object: loses the strict-parse/round-trip guarantees every other calibration record in the project has.

decisions.title: S8.6 refines dm-0066: tools/ may TYPE-import gdos output types (ArchetypeRun) and Archetype names, distinct from gate internals
decisions.why_it_exists: dm-0066 forbade tools/ from importing src/eval/gdos/ with a blanket path regex. Implementing S8.6 (tools/telemetry/DeathHeatmap.ts) surfaced that adapting a live capture into the shape analyzeTape/processCampaign consume requires naming ArchetypeRun (a DTO defined in gdos/Evidence.ts) and ArchetypeName (eval/Archetypes.ts) -- both public evaluation OUTPUT types, not scoring-gate internals. The blanket regex conflated the two, and the ToolsIsolation scan (correctly) failed.
decisions.problem_it_solves: The ToolsIsolation scan is refined to mirror GenIsolation exactly: (1) gdos SCORING-GATE internals (Emotional/Streamability/InfoDensity/Score/Economy/Curation) stay forbidden even as types -- scoring is behind judgeLevel; (2) gdos/Evidence + gdos/Report output types and eval/Archetypes names are allowed as TYPE-ONLY imports (import type, which compiles away to zero runtime coupling -- the same allowance dm-0047 grants gen/ for campaign types, and that Evidence.ts itself uses for verdict types). Value imports from gdos/ and eval/local/AgentPolicy/Evaluate/EmergentFun remain forbidden entirely; the one-way rule (nothing in src/ imports tools/) is untouched. This is a refinement that distinguishes public output types from gate internals, not a weakening -- exactly the class of pre-registered-list gap dm-0071 closed for CANONICAL_PIPELINE.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Trust that the isolation scan enforces the REAL safety property (no coupling to gate/audit internals) rather than an over-broad path match that would force telemetry to duplicate evaluation DTOs.
decisions.misconception_created: Allowing gdos/ type imports might look like a hole; the scan still blocks every value import from gdos/ and every gate-internal module, and requires the surviving gdos/Evidence + Archetypes imports to be import-type-only -- verified per file.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Duplicating ArchetypeRun/ArchetypeName as tools-local types: a second source of truth for the exact shape analyzeTape consumes, guaranteed to drift. Keeping the blanket ban and routing through a re-export: adds an indirection module for no safety gain. Building the ChapterRecord adaptation outside tools/ (in a caller): the P8 plan explicitly assigns toArchetypeRun/round-trip to S8.6, and the round-trip is the IRD P8 exit proof.

decisions.title: Adopt "Paper Collage" as the concrete realization of the REQ-070/071 Visual Grammar; map its function-accents onto the six structural categories
decisions.why_it_exists: The project had an abstract Visual Grammar (REQ-070/071: six structural categories Safe/Danger/Interactive/Temporary/Optimization/Secret, each needing a fixed palette/silhouette/motion/audio signature) but no committed concrete look. A complete art-direction asset kit (src/assets/paper-asset-library.html + src/assets/environment/) was supplied: a cut-paper/dry-ink/printed-matter style with its own function-driven accent scheme and a working procedural asset engine. A decision was needed on how the two relate before P9 authors any rendering.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Paper Collage is adopted as StylePack #1 — the concrete realization of the SAME six abstract categories, NOT a competing grammar. A mapping is fixed (docs/visual_design_bible.md §1): Safe=ink solid, Danger=terracotta sharp silhouette (doubling as the goal, split by silhouette), Interactive=dusty-teal eye/zone, Temporary=faded-pink cracked, Optimization=motion-glyphs, Secret=lavender dashed outline. CREAM/BEIGE/WASHED-BLUE are non-category environment material. Two reconciliations are deliberately left OPEN for P9 adversarial planning rather than papered over: Optimization has no dedicated accent yet (motion-glyph only), and Danger doubling as the goal must be confirmed as silhouette-separable under REQ-070 (no mixed signatures).
decisions.emotion_targeted: Confidence that the game has one coherent visual identity from day one of P9, with the abstract requirement and the concrete art kept as a single canon rather than two drifting descriptions of "how it looks".
decisions.misconception_created: Adopting a specific look may read as locking the game into paper forever; it does not — the look is a swappable StylePack (dm-0076), and the six-category GRAMMAR, not the paper MATERIALS, is the part that is permanent. REQ-070/071 stay NOT_STARTED and only flip VERIFIED when P9 implements and verifies the renderer.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Making the paper accent-scheme the new primary grammar and re-framing REQ-070/071 around it: rewrites a verified-track requirement to match one art kit and loses swappability. Leaving the art direction unrecorded until P9: the handoff repeatedly shows real design gaps are found precisely because direction is written and ledgered BEFORE code (REQ-P02), and the two open reconciliations above would have surfaced mid-implementation instead. Treating the HTML kit as the sole canon: it is authoritative for pixels but not for the rules/intent/requirement-mapping, which need a versioned doc.

decisions.title: The renderer is art-style-agnostic: a data-driven StylePack (asset-provider) seam makes the entire art style swappable with zero gameplay/logic/data changes
decisions.why_it_exists: The user requirement is that the art can be replaced with a completely different style later without touching the game. REQ-070/071 already specify the Visual Grammar be delivered "as a data-driven style system". A durable architectural rule was needed before P9 so the renderer is designed style-blind from the first slice rather than retrofitted.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Fixes the render-layer contract shape (docs/visual_design_bible.md §0): gameplay/simulation/logic (src/core, src/systems, src/gen, src/eval, tools/) stay headless and style-blind, emitting only structural category + behavior.kind + state + transform; an abstract StylePack maps (category, kind, state) to a cached visual, and swapping the pack swaps every pixel while level JSON, hitboxes, tapes, and the evaluation pipeline are untouched. Paper Collage is StylePack #1. Rendering stays strictly one-way (interpolationAlpha read-only; rendering never feeds a tick or a replay-checked value — the dm-0004 determinism axiom extended to the render layer). This decision fixes the CONTRACT; where the StylePack interface and render surface physically live, and the render-vs-tools import direction (dm-0066 one-way rule), remain P9-planning questions.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Trust that a future art overhaul is a contained, low-risk drop-in (one new StylePack) rather than a cross-cutting rewrite — the same decoupling discipline (data/logic separation, dm-0037) the headless subtrees already enjoy, now guaranteed for the visual layer.
decisions.misconception_created: A style-provider seam might look like premature abstraction for a game that ships one look; it is the literal wording of REQ-070/071 (data-driven style system) and the only structure that satisfies the stated swappability requirement, and it costs nothing at runtime (the frame loop still blits cached bitmaps).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Hardcoding Paper Collage draw calls into systems/entities: couples simulation to one art style, making a restyle a full rewrite and letting style leak into logic (violates the data-driven axiom). A build-time theme constant/flag: still bakes style choices into gameplay code paths and cannot host a structurally different art style (pixel vs. vector vs. paper) without branching logic. Deferring the seam to "whenever a second style is needed": guarantees the first renderer bakes in style assumptions that the retrofit must then unpick — exactly the drift the protocol exists to prevent.

decisions.title: Paper Collage simplification pass on the reference asset kit (~40% visual-complexity reduction, readability-first) — architecture preserved
decisions.why_it_exists: The reference asset engine (src/assets/paper-asset-library.html, the StylePack #1 source per dm-0075) was richer than gameplay readability needs and, in places, violated the very canon dm-0075 set: grain was generated per-asset in ~9 draw functions, the decor set was 16 types, jitter/subdivision were high, and the conveyor cached two directional canvases while ALSO drawing live directional chevrons. A refinement was requested to cut ~40% of visual complexity while keeping the handcrafted paper identity and the exact architecture (procedural-at-load, cached canvases, merged tile runs, one composed background, seeded determinism, drawImage-only runtime).
decisions.problem_it_solves: Applied a targeted, architecture-preserving simplification (verified in-browser: 8 decor keys, all 12 entity kinds intact, level canvas paints, zero exceptions). Changes: (1) grain is now ONE shared background layer -- per-asset grain removed so gameplay sprites are clean bold ink (better silhouettes); (2) decor set 16 -> 8 (tornPaper, graphPaper, paintBlob, brushStroke, splatter, scribble, moon, arch), variety from placement not new types; (3) generator jitter/subdivision reduced (roughPoly seg 9->14 & j 1.5->1.0, roughLine step 8->12px & j->.8, inkBlock j 2->1.4); (4) conveyor caches ONE body, direction shown by the existing live chevron sweep (removed the duplicate baked chevrons + the left/right cache split); (5) tile-run cached variants 3->2; (6) decor composited at low contrast (DECOR_ALPHA .62, anchor stain .85->.5) so it never competes with gameplay ink. Canon synced: docs/visual_design_bible.md (single-shared-grain, ~8 decor, cache-only-meaningful-states, calm jitter) + the visual-design-principle skill. No gameplay/sim/logic/schema touched; the kit stays headless reference art, rendering still gated to P9.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Clarity -- gameplay objects read instantly at small sizes because decorative noise (per-object grain, 16 decor types, high jitter) no longer competes with the silhouette. The paper feel survives via the shared background texture and preserved wear/roughness.
decisions.misconception_created: A ~40% cut might look like it weakens the art; it strengthens it against the silhouette-first principle -- less texture on gameplay, one unifying paper layer behind it, and fewer-but-better decor pieces. The reduction is in redundancy and decorative richness, not in the vocabulary or the six-category grammar.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Redesigning the renderer or switching pipelines: explicitly out of scope and would discard the working, canonized approach. Keeping per-asset grain but lowering alpha: still multiplies generation work and softens every silhouette -- a shared background layer is both cheaper and cleaner. Keeping 16 decor types but placing fewer: leaves dead asset code and tempts future over-decoration; a curated 8 with placement-driven variety is the standing rule. Caching conveyor direction as two canvases: a mere mirror is a runtime concern, and the live chevrons already encode direction -- two systems for one signal was pure redundancy.

decisions.title: One base per entity across all states + a shared 9-slice block library as the single source of structural geometry
decisions.why_it_exists: The reference kit rendered stateful entities (laser on/off, door open/closed, spring idle/launch, etc.) by seeding the WHOLE sprite from kind+state; because PaperAssets.entity mixed state.length into the seed, an entity re-rolled its hand-cut jitter per state, so the base block silhouette/proportions visibly changed between states (the reported laser·on vs laser·off inconsistency). Separately, every block-based entity generated its own rectangle geometry, duplicating the slab logic and drifting in appearance.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Two enforced rules, verified in-browser (laser base pixel-diff outside the aperture indicator = 0; only the indicator differs; all 12 kinds render, zero exceptions). (1) BASE vs STATE separation: KindDraw entries are now {base(g,T,rnd), overlay?(g,T,rnd,state)}; drawEntity seeds base from the KIND ALONE (state-independent) and the overlay from kind+state, so an entity base is byte-identical in every state and only functional overlays (indicators, beams, accents, moving parts) change. (2) A shared PaperBlocks 9-slice block library (top-left,top,top-right,left,center,right,bottom-left,bottom,bottom-right + all) built from one blockSlab() generator where only OUTER edges carry the torn jitter and inner edges stay flush, so pieces tile seamlessly into linear floors/walls/platforms. inkBlock is now a thin all-edges alias; tileRun and every block-based base (platform, laser emitter, door, conveyor, pressure plate, spring, collapsing floor, ice) draw through PaperBlocks.slab. The library is the single source of truth for structural geometry, cutting procedural duplication and maximizing cache reuse. Applies to all current and future entities. Canon synced: visual_design_bible.md (principle 9 + new block-library section) + visual-design-principle skill; a 9-slice showcase added to the library sheet. Still headless reference art; rendering gated to P9.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Trust — an object looks the same wherever and whenever it appears; state changes read as functional (the beam turns on) rather than as the whole object subtly morphing. This is the visible payoff of principle 8 (same function, same shape) extended across state.
decisions.misconception_created: Splitting base/overlay and routing through a block library may look like extra indirection; it removes duplication (one slab generator instead of per-entity rectangles), fixes a real consistency bug, and increases cache reuse — the frame loop is unchanged (still cached-bitmap blits).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Seeding the whole sprite from a state-independent key: keeps state visuals frozen too (a closed door and open door would share the same inner panel jitter is fine, but on/off indicators must differ) — the clean split is base-seeded-by-kind + overlay-seeded-by-kind+state. Leaving each entity to draw its own rectangle but fixing only the seed: fixes the immediate bug but leaves structural geometry duplicated and free to drift; a shared library is the durable single source of truth the user asked for. A full nine separate cached corner/edge canvases per entity base: unnecessary — free-standing bases use the all-edges slab; the 9-slice variants serve tiled floors/walls/platforms and the showcase.

decisions.title: Connected blocks render as one seamless paper mass: roughness/wear on external boundaries only; terrain composes as a single silhouette
decisions.why_it_exists: Adjacent block-based assets showed visible gaps: white cracks and uneven overlaps between neighbours. Causes were (a) blockSlab applied wear across the whole slab including edges where blocks touch, (b) inner edges sat exactly on the tile boundary so sub-pixel anti-aliasing left a light seam between two abutting opaque fills, and (c) the tilemap drew per-row runs that abutted rather than merging into one shape, so vertical/corner joins seamed. The target look (image-3, Limbo/GRIS-style) is a continuous torn-paper cut-out with roughness only on the outer silhouette.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Three enforced rules, verified in-browser (internal vertical seam holes 0/74, internal horizontal seam holes 0/74; external bottom edge solid-fraction 0.51 = still torn). (1) Roughness/jitter/wear on EXTERNAL boundaries only: in blockSlab an inner (touching) edge stays dead-straight, un-inset, and wear is CLIPPED to keep a solid margin off every inner edge; only exposed edges get jitter, the dry-brush top highlight, and wear. (2) Inner edges BLEED ~1px past the boundary so neighbours overlap (opaque-over-opaque is invisible), killing the AA crack. (3) Connected terrain composes as a SINGLE silhouette, not stacked tiles: fill every solid cell opaque (overlapping neighbours 1px), then apply the torn edge (destination-out ragged bites) + wear + top highlight ONLY where a cell faces empty space. The tilemap now blits one TERRAIN canvas instead of per-row runs; PaperAssets.tileRun (now redundant) was removed so the silhouette compositor is the single path for terrain. Applies to all block-based assets and future entities. Canon synced: visual_design_bible.md (block-library seam rules) + visual-design-principle skill + PKG render/renderer note.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Solidity — a wall or floor reads as one hand-torn object you could pick up, not a grid of bricks with glue lines. The handmade look survives (edges are still irregular) while structural continuity is absolute.
decisions.misconception_created: Merging terrain into one silhouette might look like it abandons the block library; it does not — the library is still the single source of the slab look and the 9-slice pieces, and the silhouette compositor applies the exact same "rough outer edges only" rule at level scale. It is also strictly better for the "one bitmap per level" performance habit.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Keeping per-tile/per-run blocks and just nudging them together: abutting opaque fills still crack under anti-aliasing, and wear at the join still erodes it — only overlap+internal-wear-exclusion removes seams reliably. Drawing seams and then painting over them: fights the symptom, not the cause, and is fragile at corners. A single opaque rect with no edge treatment: seamless but loses the hand-cut paper aesthetic the whole style depends on — the torn-outer-edge-only approach keeps both.

decisions.title: Modular seamless terrain tile system (PaperTiles) + clean-solid structural masses; fixes block seams and the noisy-black art deviation
decisions.why_it_exists: Two linked problems surfaced. (1) Adjacent procedural blocks showed visible seams/cracks and per-tile silhouettes because each run/tile was an independent rough slab with its own jitter, wear and padding. (2) In fixing that mechanically the terrain drifted from the committed art direction: destination-out wear/tornBite plus per-object interior wear filled the black masses with light specks and a thick grainy torn frame, whereas the target (reference image, Limbo/GRIS style) is a CLEAN solid black mass with a torn edge only on the exposed silhouette. The user asked for a proper production-quality modular tileset.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Introduced PaperTiles: a modular tile construction kit where every tile is a T-by-T cell with an identical square collision box and only EXPOSED surfaces are drawn. A tile fills its cell with clean solid ink; edges facing empty space get a gently torn outline whose irregularity tapers to 0 at the shared corners; edges touching a neighbour stay dead-straight and flush; tiles erode INWARD only so they never overhang. Terrain autotiles by a 4-neighbour mask (out-of-bounds = solid, so the map frame outer edge is flush and only edges facing the play area are torn) and composes by painting tile polygons DIRECTLY into one bitmap (same coordinate space, not per-tile drawImage) so there is no sub-pixel sampling seam. The kit exposes the full named set: center, four edges, four outer corners, isolated block, floating-platform ends (left/mid/right), vertical-wall pieces (top/mid/bottom). Structural cleanliness: removed all interior wear/grain from blockSlab and terrain -- solid masses are clean ink, only the exposed outline is torn; wear/grain remain on decor and the single shared background layer. Verified headlessly (browser MCP offline): tile-geometry invariants pass -- deterministic; every tile includes exact corner points (seamless corners); no point overhangs the cell (no overlap); internal edges flush at the exact boundary (contiguous fill); center mask = perfect square; named kit covers 16 distinct masks. Canon synced: visual_design_bible.md (principle 6 clean-solid + rewritten seamless section as the tile system) + visual-design-principle skill; a TERRAIN TILE SYSTEM showcase added to the asset library. Still headless reference art; rendering gated to P9.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Trust that terrain reads as one solid, deliberately hand-cut object -- the clean Limbo/GRIS silhouette -- not a grid of speckly bricks; and that the tileset behaves like a production kit where arbitrary combinations just work.
decisions.misconception_created: A full autotile mask system may look heavier than merged runs; it is the correct model for seamless arbitrary structures and composes to the same single cached bitmap, and removing interior wear makes it cheaper, not costlier. Pixel-accurate visual re-verification in-browser is still pending (the browser MCP disconnected mid-session); the geometry invariants are proven headlessly.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Keeping the destination-out tornBite/1px-overlap silhouette: it erodes both close edges of a thin frame and leaves specks -- the noisy black the user rejected. Per-tile cached tiles blitted with drawImage: risks a sub-pixel AA seam between abutting images; painting polygons directly into one canvas removes that entirely. Jittering exposed edges without pinning corners to 0: adjacent tiles would not meet -- re-introduces cracks. Merged rough runs (the prior approach): give per-run silhouettes and cannot express corners/inner-corners/islands the user needs. Retaining interior wear for paper texture: it is exactly what made the solid masses look noisy; the torn silhouette plus the shared background grain carry the paper identity without dirtying the mass.

decisions.title: P9 render layer lives in a new top-level render/ subtree, one-way-isolated above src/ and tools/; render/tooling/ is the only area importing tools/
decisions.why_it_exists: P9 must render both the game (needs src/ state) and the P8 editor/debug tooling (needs tools/ types). Every existing home violates an isolation rule: src/render/ importing tools/ breaks the dm-0066 one-way rule (nothing under src/ imports tools/), and rendering inside tools/ breaks the dm-0065 split that ToolsIsolation.test.ts scan-enforces (no canvas/DOM/WebGL under tools/). The handoff and the PKG PlannedSystemNodes explicitly deferred this location decision to P9 planning.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Gives the presentation layer a legal address at the TOP of the dependency chain: render/ may import src/ (public seams only) and tools/ (as a typed library), and nothing under src/ or tools/ may ever import render/. Within the layer, render/tooling/ is the sole area permitted to import tools/, so the game shell stays tools-free. Scan-enforced from the first render/**/*.ts module by test/unit/RenderIsolation.test.ts (the dm-0053 lesson: isolation rules get scans at subtree birth, never prose-only). tsconfig include and directory_structure.md gain the subtree; the PKG render/* pending nodes materialize under the corrected paths.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (architecture decision).
decisions.misconception_created: The PKG pending nodes provisionally said src/render/StylePack.ts; those paths were provisional and are corrected at S9.1. Also: render/ importing tools/ is legal ONLY in render/tooling/ — a future session must not read this as a blanket license.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: src/render/ rejected (editor UI could not import EditorDraft/Overlay descriptors without violating dm-0066). tools/render/ rejected (breaks dm-0065; tools/ is a stable scan-enforced pure-logic subtree). Splitting game-render under src/ and tool-render elsewhere rejected (two half-renderers, duplicated batching/executor, and the grammar/pack seam would span roots).

decisions.title: P9 surface resolution: Canvas2D rasterizes at load behind the StylePack; WebGL2 runs the frame loop; real time enters simulation only via Clock.advance
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-170 and the IRD name a WebGL renderer with batching, while the visual design bible (section 5) states the Paper Collage performance contract in Canvas2D idiom (cached canvases, drawImage blits, one background bitmap, source-rect crop) and section 6 defers the canvas-vs-WebGL choice to P9 planning. Separately, a render loop needs requestAnimationFrame/wall-clock, which dm-0067 scoped to tools/profiler/ only.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Resolves both tensions. (1) The two idioms describe different MOMENTS: the StylePack rasterizes procedural art via Canvas2D at exactly the bible's three sanctioned moments (asset creation, level load, appearance change) into cached bitmaps; bitmaps are atlas-packed/uploaded once; the frame loop is WebGL2 batched textured quads — "drawImage of cached canvases" maps 1:1 to "textured-quad draws of cached rasters", "one background bitmap + source-rect crop" maps to one background texture with UV crop, and "generate never per-frame" survives verbatim. (2) The wall-clock exception extends to render/ with a precise contract: real time enters simulation ONLY as the realDeltaSeconds argument of the P1 Clock.advance seam (banked into whole fixed steps); rendering is a pure projection (state, previous, interpolationAlpha, grammar, pack, viewport) -> DrawList and never writes into WorldState or any replay-checked value. The scan amends to "tools/profiler/ + render/ only"; src/ stays clean.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (architecture decision).
decisions.misconception_created: WebGL2 in the frame loop does NOT mean procedural drawing moved to shaders — generation stays Canvas2D behind the pack. And the render loop reading real time does NOT weaken determinism: a headless run fed the same InputFrames must stay bit-identical (test-asserted at S9.8).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Canvas2D frame loop rejected (fails REQ-170's explicit WebGL wording and forfeits the instanced batching REQ-162 names). WebGL1 fallback rejected (WebGL2 is the universal evergreen baseline in mid-2026; a fallback is permanently-dead code). WebGPU backend rejected (REQ-170 names WebGL; portal long-tail hardware; the Gl2Device seam keeps a future backend additive, not a rewrite).

decisions.title: Grammar/pack split: the six-category Visual Grammar is style-agnostic versioned data; all materials live behind the StylePack; the mixing prohibition is enforced structurally at both layers
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-070/071 demand the grammar be a data-driven style system with mixing prohibited, and dm-0076 mandates the art style be swappable with zero gameplay/data changes. Those combine into a precise split the naive slice table never made: what is stable across StylePacks versus what a pack owns.
decisions.problem_it_solves: The grammar — six category ids, behavior.kind -> category bindings, per-category criticality (danger structurally always-critical), and the signature-role contract (palette/silhouette/motion/audio) — is versioned strict-parsed data (the sixth versioned schema, after ScoringProfile/CampaignProfile/GenProfile/TelemetryProfile and the level schema). Materials (paper palette hexes, slab/tile generators, decor, synth patches) live behind the StylePack interface. REQ-070 mixing is rejected twice structurally: the grammar parser rejects double bindings, and validatePack rejects incomplete or overlapping per-category signatures (including terracotta appearing outside Danger). Audio signatures are procedural synth patches (waveform + envelopes) as pack DATA — zero binary audio assets in P9; P10 samples would flow through the S9.7 manifest later. A future StylePack replaces materials only; grammar, bindings, level data, and tapes are untouched.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Player trust: one signature always means one thing, in any art style.
decisions.misconception_created: The grammar schema does NOT contain colors. A hex in render/grammar/ is a defect; colors exist only inside a pack.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: A single "StyleProfile" holding both grammar and Paper Collage materials (the pre-canon draft design) rejected: swapping art would rewrite the grammar file, violating dm-0076. Enforcing mixing prohibition by review/convention rejected: every other invariant in this project is scan- or parser-enforced; conventions drift.

decisions.title: Grammar reconciliation: Optimization's signature is an achromatic ink/cream motion-glyph layer — no new accent colour; terracotta glyphs become a pack-validation error
decisions.why_it_exists: The bible (section 1) flags Optimization as having no dedicated paper accent — the kit expresses it via motion glyphs, currently drawn in terracotta (conveyor chevrons), which collides with Danger's accent. Flagged explicitly for P9-planning resolution.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Fixes Optimization's four channels without palette inflation: palette = ink/cream glyphs only (the ABSENCE of chroma plus motion IS the palette signature — no other category is achromatic-kinetic), silhouette = chevron / double-arrow glyph family, motion = directional sweep, audio = directional whoosh patch. The PaperStylePack port recolours the kit's terracotta chevrons to ink/cream; validatePack rejects terracotta outside Danger. Colour-blind-safe by construction (motion + glyph shape carry the meaning, honouring REQ-016's never-colour-alone rule), and terracotta tightens toward meaning exactly "lethal".
decisions.emotion_targeted: Skill-route invitation without threat: optimization elements should read as "faster if you dare", never "this kills you".
decisions.misconception_created: Optimization having no accent colour is a SIGNATURE, not a gap — a future session must not "helpfully" assign it a colour; that would re-open the collision this decision closes.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: A dedicated seventh accent rejected: the palette is deliberately tiny (5 accents + 3 supporting), every candidate hue either collided with an existing meaning (washed blue = cold/slippery environment) or read as decor, and the least-common category least deserves palette budget. Keeping terracotta glyphs rejected: actively misleading under REQ-016 (a lethal-coloured accent on a non-lethal conveyor).

decisions.title: Grammar reconciliation: the goal unbinds from Danger and binds to Interactive (dusty teal); terracotta means exactly "kills you"
decisions.why_it_exists: The bible (section 1) flags that terracotta currently marks both "kills you" and "the flag", separated only by silhouette, and requires P9 planning to confirm silhouette-only separation or split the goal's signature. REQ-070 is strict (no signature mixing) and REQ-016 wants failure information unambiguous.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Binds the goal to Interactive in the grammar data: taxonomically honest (sim-side the goal IS the terminal proximity trigger, and Interactive already means "logic that responds to your arrival" — zones, triggers, watchers, plates), emotionally right (teal reads calm/inviting against terracotta threat), and doubly separable for colour-blind players (silhouette AND colour). Danger's meaning tightens to exactly "kills you", strengthening REQ-016's "terracotta = failure information". A data-level change (one binding + pack pennant recolour), cheap to revisit if the art-direction owner overrules.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Commitment clarity at the moment of the one jump: the player mid-air must never confuse "this saves me" with "this kills me".
decisions.misconception_created: The kit's reference sheet still shows a terracotta flag until the S9.2 port lands; the grammar data is authoritative from S9.1. Also: Secret ("exits") does NOT claim the main goal — Secret's exits are hidden/rule-change exits only.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Keeping the terracotta goal on silhouette-only separation rejected: the bible's "commitment target" unification (the jump commits you toward death or goal alike) is genuinely poetic for a one-jump game, but it is a signature mix under REQ-070's strict wording and dilutes REQ-016. Binding the goal to Secret/lavender rejected: the main goal is neither hidden nor a rule-change, and a dashed "idea of a thing" outline on the primary objective misleads.

decisions.title: P9 testing/dependency strategy: hexagonal device seams with hand-rolled fakes; zero new npm packages; Poki SDK via official CDN with a typed PortalSdk adapter; slice table restructured 7 -> 11
decisions.why_it_exists: The DoD requires node --test proof but P9 targets browser APIs (WebGL2, WebAudio, Canvas2D, rAF, the PokiSDK global). The dm-0038 package policy requires latest-stable pins for anything used, re-verified at implementation date. The naive seven-slice table omitted the inherited REQ-130/131/132 shares, REQ-150 visual sub-clauses, REQ-016 visual share, REQ-001/002, and StylePack #1 itself.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Every render-layer module is pure logic against injected minimal ENUMERATED interfaces — Raster2D, Gl2Device, AudioDevice, AssetFetcher, PortalSdk, FrameScheduler — with hand-rolled recording fakes in tests; only render/platform/ binding modules may name browser globals (scan-enforced). Pack tests assert STRUCTURE, not pixels: identical command streams per identity seed, base byte-identical across states (dm-0078), no wear/grain on interiors (dm-0080), cache hits on repeat keys. Package policy re-verified 2026-07-11: typescript 7.0.2 and @types/node 26.1.1 are the exact latest stable on registry.npmjs.org; P9 adds ZERO new npm dependencies. Poki integration follows the official 2026 approach verified against sdk.poki.com: CDN script game-cdn.poki.com/scripts/v2/poki-sdk.js, init -> gameLoadingFinished -> gameplayStart/Stop -> commercialBreak/rewardedBreak, with audio+keyboard disabled during breaks and space/arrow default-scroll prevented — wrapped behind PortalSdk with a NullPortalSdk for dev/test. The slice table is restructured to eleven slices covering every owned and inherited share.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (engineering strategy).
decisions.misconception_created: Fakes passing does not equal browser-proven: real-browser smoke is EXPLICITLY deferred to P11's build pipeline (REQ-172) and S9.11's report must not overclaim. The interfaces are minimal subsets — extending one means enumerating and documenting the new member, never widening to the whole DOM type.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: jsdom/headless-gl rejected (native/heavyweight deps, drift risk, and structural assertions cover the logic; the project culture is hand-rolled fakes). pixi/three rejected (framework batching hides the REQ-161/162 engineering P9 must own and prove). @poki/sdk@0.0.4 rejected (official but pre-1.0 forwarder around the very global we wrap behind an injected seam anyway; the policy pins latest STABLE releases).

decisions.title: REQ-174 added: non-IAP rewarded-ad level-skip / alternative-route monetization, hooked through Poki rewardedBreak
decisions.why_it_exists: User direction (2026-07-11): the product needs a monetization path, explicitly ad-based rather than in-app purchases — letting a player watch a rewarded ad to skip a level or reveal an alternative route/hint. No existing REQ named a monetization mechanic; REQ-170 only covers the generic Poki SDK/ad-placement lifecycle hooks, not a specific in-game monetization flow.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Backlogs the mechanic as REQ-174 (§18), phase column P9,P10,P11: P9 builds the rewardedBreak hook as part of its already-planned PortalSdk seam (S9.8) — pure infrastructure, no new P9 slice needed since dm-0082/dm-0086 already put commercialBreak/rewardedBreak behind PortalSdk. P10 decides and wires the actual in-game skip/alt-route flow (what "skip" means for a one-jump puzzle — likely: unlock the next level without solving this one, or reveal a hint/alternate route overlay) as part of authoring campaign flow. P11 gives it a release-audit share alongside the other REQ-132/163 hardening. IRD P9/P10/P11 sections and the backlog updated accordingly.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Player goodwill: an optional, transparent ad-for-convenience trade, never a paywall — preserves the "no arbitrary trolling" fairness axiom (REQ-016) by making the skip strictly optional.
decisions.misconception_created: REQ-174 is NOT built in P9 — P9 only builds the generic rewardedBreak hook (already implicit in the S9.8 PortalSdk design); the actual skip/alt-route GAMEPLAY FLOW is P10-owned and unbuilt. A future session must not assume a skip button exists until P10 delivers it.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: In-app purchases considered and explicitly rejected per the user's direct instruction ("no in app purchase etc") — REQ-174's wording hard-codes the non-IAP constraint so a later session cannot quietly add a paid-currency system.

decisions.title: REQ-084 added: campaign-wide difficulty distribution (Easy 20% / Medium 35% / Hard 25% / Harder 15% / Very Hard 5%)
decisions.why_it_exists: User direction (2026-07-11) specified an exact difficulty-tier proportion for the authored level pool, with an explicit purpose per tier (teach mechanics/build confidence; core clever-betrayal experience; mechanic-combination troll moments; complex deception for dedicated players; endgame creator-bait mastery). No existing REQ captured a campaign-wide distribution target — REQ-013 only says "difficulty from player decisions", and REQ-142's macro curriculum validation checks trajectory health, not tier proportions.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Backlogs the exact percentages as REQ-084 (§9, next to REQ-083's chapter architecture), P10-owned. This is a CALIBRATION target for content authoring, not a code change: P10's level-manufacturing pipeline (P7, already built) and chapter architecture (REQ-083) must, in aggregate across the full campaign, land levels into difficulty tiers at roughly these proportions; REQ-142's macro curriculum validation is the natural enforcement point (its own IRD exit-condition line amended to check tier proportions against REQ-084 within calibrated tolerance). No src/gen/ or src/eval/ code changes now — those subtrees are P7/P4-verified and P9 must not touch them; P10 wires this when it authors real content.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Player retention arc: casual players hook and stay confident (Easy/Medium = 55%); the long tail (Harder/Very Hard = 20%) is explicitly "creator bait" for streamers/completionists, not a barrier most players must clear.
decisions.misconception_created: REQ-084 is a TARGET DISTRIBUTION for the finished campaign, not a per-chapter or per-level rule — a future session must not force every chapter individually into these exact proportions; the tolerance and aggregation granularity (whole-campaign vs. per-chapter) is an open decision for P10 planning.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Hardcoding the percentages into gen/ or eval/ logic rejected outright — violates the data-driven invariant (no gameplay values hardcoded in logic) and both subtrees are verified-stable; the distribution is P10-authoring calibration data instead.

decisions.title: Death-feedback timing tension flagged: Lifecycle.ts reloads the very next tick after defeat, leaving exactly one renderable defeated snapshot — resolution deferred to S9.5, not decided unilaterally
decisions.why_it_exists: User direction (2026-07-11): a wrong jump should "play death graphics instantly and restart the level" — prompted a zero-assumption read of the existing P3-verified src/systems/Lifecycle.ts (dm-0018) before assuming the request needed new sim code. Lifecycle already reloads on the tick immediately following a defeated runState (REQ-003's "instant gameplay iteration"), so instant restart is ALREADY built and P3-verified. But that same immediacy means only ONE committed WorldState snapshot ever carries runState==='defeated' before instantiateWorld replaces it — at FIXED_STEP_SECONDS (1/60s) that is one-to-a-few real display frames, which may be too short for a death graphic to read as satisfying.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Records the finding without touching Lifecycle.ts (a P3-VERIFIED module; the protocol's anchor-safety rule requires dependency-mapping + signature-freezing a verified system before modifying it, not a silent edit mid-P9). Three options are laid out as an explicit P9 open question (execution_plan.md, carried to S9.5): (a) a render-side presentation hold — the shell keeps showing the defeated snapshot for a few extra rendered frames purely at the presentation layer, zero sim/replay impact, the dm-0082 projection-purity contract intact; (b) a sim-side grace-tick count before Lifecycle reloads, which IS a Lifecycle.ts change and needs its own anchor-safety pass; (c) accept the single-tick window and lean on the defeat marker's silhouette/motion (bible's silhouette-first principle) to read instantly rather than animate over time. S9.5 (game feel + fairness visuals) is where this gets decided and ledgered for real, once the render layer exists to prototype against.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Fair, legible failure (REQ-016): the player must be able to SEE why they died, not just feel the level snap back before they process it.
decisions.misconception_created: This decision does NOT pick option (a), (b), or (c) — a future session must not assume the render hold is already decided; S9.5 must actually resolve it, not treat this ledger entry as the resolution.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Deciding the resolution immediately (mid-S9.1, before any render code exists to prototype the hold duration against) was rejected as premature — S9.5 is where game-feel timing is tuned against real DrawList/frame-time data, not planning-time guesswork.

decisions.title: StylePack interface amended with rasterize(request, device): void, plus a determinism probe in validatePack
decisions.why_it_exists: S9.1 shipped visual() as pure cache-identity management (bitmap handle + anchor + pad) but never specified how a bitmap actually gets DRAWN — the atlas (S9.4) needs to turn a handle into real pixels exactly once. Without a dedicated method, either visual() would have to both assign identity AND draw (conflating two concerns) or drawing would have no seam at all.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Adds StylePack.rasterize(request, device: Raster2D): void — a pure, side-effecting function that issues the deterministic draw-command sequence for a request into an injected device, called by the atlas exactly once per distinct bitmap id. validatePack (render/style/StylePack.ts) gained a determinism probe: for every bound role, it creates two independent trace recorders (render/style/Raster2D.ts's createTraceRecorder), calls rasterize twice with the identical request, and asserts the command traces are byte-identical — catching a pack that (incorrectly) reads wall-clock time or Math.random inside a draw function. This is an in-progress-phase interface amendment (S9.1 is COMPLETED, not yet VERIFIED — REQ-070/071 flip only at S9.11), which the four-state discipline explicitly allows.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (architecture decision).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session must not assume visual() alone is sufficient to get pixels — rasterize() is the only method that actually draws; visual() only manages cache identity/sizing/anchor.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Folding drawing into visual() itself rejected: conflates pure identity/cache management with a side-effecting device call, and would force every visual() caller (including validatePack's cache-by-key probe) to supply a device even when only checking identity.

decisions.title: dm-0084 correction: the reference kit's Optimization elements (movingPlatform, conveyor) were ALREADY achromatic — no terracotta recolor was needed
decisions.why_it_exists: dm-0084 (P9 planning, before S9.2 read the reference file in full) stated "the kit expresses optimization through motion glyphs... currently drawn in terracotta (conveyor chevrons)" and planned to recolor them to ink/cream. Zero-assumption reading of src/assets/paper-asset-library.html at S9.2 (KindDraw.movingPlatform's ↔ glyph: g.fillStyle=INK; the runtime conveyor chevron sweep: rgba(236,229,212,.9) ≈ CREAM) shows neither element ever used a terracotta accent — the planning-time claim was inaccurate.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Records the correction so a future session does not go looking for a recolor commit that never needed to happen. dm-0084's RESOLUTION still holds and required zero extra work: KindDraw.ts (S9.2) carries movingPlatform's ink glyph and conveyor's ink/cream body forward UNCHANGED from the reference sheet, and PaperStylePack.paletteAccent('optimization') === null (achromatic) is enforced structurally by validatePack regardless of what any single entity's hand-authored colours happen to be. The conveyor's LIVE animated chevron sweep (time-driven, drawn outside KindDraw in the reference's render() loop) is explicitly out of S9.2's scope — it belongs to the scene compiler (S9.3) via MotionSpec('optimization').motionClass==='sweep', not a cached bitmap.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (correction of a planning-time factual claim).
decisions.misconception_created: None — this entry exists specifically to prevent one (a phantom recolor task).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Leaving dm-0084's inaccurate rationale text uncorrected was rejected — the ledger is the project's long-term memory; an unflagged factual error there would resurface as confusion in a much later session with no code context to catch it.

decisions.title: PaperStylePack ignores VisualRequest.identitySeed for every S9.2 role (entities, terrain, player, goal) — cache key is role:state:T alone
decisions.why_it_exists: The S9.1 VisualRequest doc comment says "same identity ⇒ same seed ⇒ identical visual", written to match the visual design bible's general §4 language ("seeded RNG hashed off entity identity/grid position — unique per object"). But dm-0078 (pre-P9 art-canon session) already established that an entity's BASE geometry is seeded from KIND ALONE, never per-instance identity — the fix for the laser on/off silhouette-drift bug. Reading the reference engine's actual drawEntity() at S9.2 confirms this literally: base(g,T,RNG(hashSeed(900,kind))) — no identity or grid position enters the seed at all, for any of the twelve kinds, ever.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Clarifies WHY, without contradicting either dm-0078 or the bible: bible principle 8 ("same function, same shape, everywhere") means gameplay entities are VOCABULARY — a spike must look identical everywhere so the player's learned visual grammar transfers instantly, which is the OPPOSITE of per-instance uniqueness. The bible's "unique per object" language is correctly scoped to DECOR (out of S9.2's scope, PaperAssets.decor(name,w,h,seed) takes a real per-placement seed in the reference) and TERRAIN TILE VARIANT selection (a small position-hashed set, already encoded into PaperStylePack's terrain `state` string by the caller, not identitySeed). PaperStylePack therefore legitimately ignores identitySeed for all its current roles — this is not a bug, and is now pinned by a dedicated regression test (PaperStylePack.test.ts).
decisions.emotion_targeted: Visual-vocabulary consistency: a player who has learned "triangles = death" must never see a differently-jittered spike and wonder if it means something else.
decisions.misconception_created: A future StylePack (or a future role added to this one, e.g. decor once it enters scope) MAY legitimately use identitySeed for real per-placement variation — this decision applies only to the specific roles PaperStylePack currently serves, not to the StylePack interface's contract in general.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Threading identitySeed into entity base geometry (to literally honor the interface doc's "same identity ⇒ same seed" wording for every role) was rejected outright — it would reintroduce exactly the class of bug dm-0078 fixed, this time varying by WORLD POSITION instead of by STATE, defeating both cache reuse and the vocabulary-consistency principle.

decisions.title: SceneCompiler derives pressurePlate/spring overlay state from nothing (default/idle) — no live "currently pressed/launching" signal exists in WorldState yet
decisions.why_it_exists: KindDraw.pressurePlate and KindDraw.spring both have an overlay() that branches on a live contact/launch signal (the reference engine faked this with a wall-clock sine pulse for demo purposes). Reading src/entities/World.ts's EntityState at S9.3 confirms no such per-entity "isPressed"/"isLaunching" field is exposed today — that would require the scene compiler to re-derive Sensors/trigger evaluation, which is gameplay logic the render layer has no business re-implementing (render/ reaches src/eval/ only via AgentHarness, and reaches src/systems/ only via its own public read paths — never by rebuilding a system's internals).
decisions.problem_it_solves: SceneCompiler.entityState() returns 'default' for these two kinds, which KindDraw resolves to their idle/unpressed overlay. This is scoped and documented (SceneCompiler.ts's own header comment), not a silent gap: a future slice that wants a live-pressed pulse must either (a) add a genuinely new WorldState field written by the physics/trigger system itself (the correct place for gameplay state to originate) and read it here read-only, or (b) accept that pressurePlate/spring are visually static until then. Neither pressurePlate nor spring gained a fake render-side re-derivation of contact logic.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (scope honesty).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session must not "fix" this by computing proximity/contact directly inside SceneCompiler.ts (that would duplicate Sensors.ts logic in the render layer, a system-isolation violation) — the fix, if wanted, is a new read-only WorldState field.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Re-deriving contact/press state locally inside the scene compiler (e.g., checking AABB overlap between the player and the plate) was rejected: it would silently duplicate physics/sensor logic the simulation already owns, and could drift from the real gameplay answer (e.g., a plate held down by a non-player entity would render wrong).

decisions.title: Entity culling queries the quadtree against CURRENT (pre-interpolation) positions, not the alpha-blended render position
decisions.why_it_exists: compileScene interpolates each visible entity's DRAWN position between previous and current snapshots by alpha, but culling (which entities even get considered) happens via the P3 SpatialPartition quadtree, which is built from a single WorldState (REQ-162's existing, verified API) — there is no interpolated-quadtree variant, and building one would mean either reimplementing the quadtree over blended coordinates (duplicating P3's tree logic) or querying twice and unioning results.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Documents the deliberate simplification: culling queries against `current` world positions only. A fast-moving entity within roughly one tick's travel of the viewport boundary could theoretically pop in/out up to half a tick earlier/later than its true interpolated position would justify — imperceptible at 60 fixed steps/second for anything but an extreme edge case, and viewpoints in practice have margin beyond the strict visible rect. Noted as an open item rather than solved with extra machinery that has no driving requirement yet.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (performance/correctness tradeoff, documented).
decisions.misconception_created: None intended — flagged explicitly in SceneCompiler.ts's own comment so a future session revisiting culling precision does not need to rediscover this from scratch.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Querying the quadtree against BOTH previous and current positions and unioning the index sets was considered and deferred: doubles culling cost for a correctness gain with no reported symptom (no test or REQ currently demands sub-tick cull precision); revisit only if a real pop-in artifact is observed with actual gameplay content (P10).

decisions.title: DrawItem amended to carry the originating VisualRequest alongside its BitmapHandle
decisions.why_it_exists: S9.4's Atlas/Batcher need to call StylePack.rasterize(request, device) the first time a given bitmap id is seen, to actually produce its pixels for GPU upload. S9.3's DrawItem only carried the resolved BitmapHandle (id/width/height) — enough for cache identity, but not enough to draw from scratch; the CachedVisual/BitmapHandle contract was deliberately opaque (S9.1) and never meant to be re-derivable from itself.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Adds DrawItem.request: VisualRequest (plain data, JSON-serializable — no interface break to the existing JSON round-trip test, which still passes deepEqual after re-serialization). Atlas.ensureRegion's producePixels thunk closes over item.request to call pack.rasterize on first sight of a bitmap id, memoized thereafter. This is an in-progress-phase amendment to a COMPLETED-not-VERIFIED S9.3 module, the same class of allowed change dm-0090 made to StylePack.ts at S9.2.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (architecture decision).
decisions.misconception_created: None — DrawItem.request is inert data for any consumer that does not need to rasterize (e.g., a hypothetical debug overlay reading DrawItems); only the atlas/batcher use it.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: A side-channel Map<bitmapId, VisualRequest> threaded separately alongside the DrawList was rejected: it would require SceneCompiler and the batcher to agree on a second parallel data structure staying in sync with the DrawList, for no benefit over just carrying the field on the item itself.

decisions.title: S9.4 batches per-sprite instances via GPU instancing; a fully pre-composed "one background bitmap per level" merge is explicitly deferred, not built
decisions.why_it_exists: The visual design bible §5 and the original P9 execution-plan prose describe "one background bitmap per level" (a single pre-composed canvas the camera crops) as the target performance shape for Canvas2D-idiom engines. S9.4 instead batches same-atlas-page sprite instances (terrain tiles, entities, player, goal) via WebGL2 instanced draw calls — dozens of identical-mask terrain tiles share one atlas region and one page, collapsing into ONE drawInstanced call, which is a real, tested, working form of "render batching for static geometry" (REQ-162's own wording) even though it is architecturally instancing rather than a merged mega-texture.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Prevents S9.4's verification report from overclaiming a "one background bitmap" deliverable that was not actually built. REQ-162 is satisfied by the tested instanced-batching win (GlExecutor.test.ts proves batches.length < drawList.length and exactly one drawInstanced call per batch); a full background pre-composition pass remains a legitimate FUTURE optimization (further reducing page/texture-switch overhead for very large static scenes) that no current test or REQ actually requires. Noted explicitly so a later session does not assume it already exists.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (scope honesty under time constraint).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session must not assume a single "background texture" concept exists anywhere in render/gl/ — there is none; static geometry batching happens via atlas-page grouping + instancing only.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Building full background pre-composition now was weighed and deferred: it requires a genuine Raster2D-to-pixels compositing pass across the WHOLE visible/level terrain into one canvas-sized bitmap, which only becomes possible once render/platform/ (S9.8) binds a real 2D context — attempting it headlessly now would mean inventing a second, throwaway pixel-compositing mechanism purely to satisfy a test, which is exactly the kind of hollow, spec-chasing work the no-placeholder discipline exists to prevent.

decisions.title: dm-0089 resolved: the defeat marker's screen time comes from the existing fixed-step/render decoupling, not a new hold mechanism
decisions.why_it_exists: dm-0089 (S9.1 planning) flagged that Lifecycle.ts reloads on the very next tick after defeat, leaving exactly one renderable "defeated" WorldState snapshot, and asked S9.5 to decide between a render-side hold, a sim-side grace-tick (a Lifecycle.ts change), or accepting the single-tick window. S9.5 (DefeatMarker.ts) had to actually pick one to ship REQ-016's defeat marker.
decisions.problem_it_solves: No new machinery is needed: the shell's frame loop (S9.8, not yet built, but already specified in the P9 plan) renders every real display frame by calling compileScene against the CURRENT committed tick pair at a computed interpolationAlpha — it does not require a new sim tick to render again. Between the tick where runState flips to 'defeated' and the next fixed step (which reloads), an arbitrary number of real display frames CAN render that same defeated snapshot (zero at minimum only if the display refresh is slower than the fixed step, which is not the target hardware profile) with the marker present and critical=true every time deriveDefeatMarker is called. This is option (a) from dm-0089, chosen because it needed zero new state and zero touching of the P3-VERIFIED Lifecycle.ts. If playtesting (P10+) later shows this reads as too brief on very high fixed-step-relative-to-refresh-rate configurations, option (b) — a genuine sim-side grace-tick — remains available as a documented fallback, gated behind Lifecycle.ts's own anchor-safety pass.
decisions.emotion_targeted: Fair, legible failure (REQ-016): the player must see why they died before the world resets under them.
decisions.misconception_created: A future session must not assume the marker is GUARANTEED to render for a fixed minimum real-world duration — it renders for as many real frames as the shell happens to draw before the next fixed step, which is a display-refresh-rate-dependent quantity, not a designed minimum. If a minimum hold time becomes a real requirement, that is option (b), not this decision.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: A sim-side grace-tick (option b) was rejected for THIS slice: it requires modifying Lifecycle.ts (P3-VERIFIED), which the protocol requires a dependency-map + signature-freeze pass for — disproportionate for a decision that the render-loop's existing structure already resolves for free. Revisit only if real playtesting surfaces an actual complaint.

decisions.title: Bug fix: PaperStylePack.stateMatters() incorrectly excluded the player role from state-sensitive caching, collapsing idle/prejump/air into one bitmap
decisions.why_it_exists: S9.2's stateMatters() classified player alongside goal as state-invariant ("goal is always the one flag, no state variants") — but the player genuinely has three distinguishable poses (idle/prejump/air) that drawPlayer() renders differently. Because visual()'s cache key normalized state to 'default' whenever stateMatters() returned false, every player pose collapsed onto the SAME cached bitmap, silently defeating REQ-150's anticipation-frame requirement (the prejump squat would never actually render — the player would always look idle). Caught by S9.5's own integration test (GameFeel.test.ts), which was written specifically to prove the anticipation-frame REQ end-to-end rather than trusting that S9.2/S9.3's unit tests implied it.
decisions.problem_it_solves: stateMatters('player') now returns true, so the cache key includes the real pose string and visual()/rasterize() produce (and cache) three genuinely distinct bitmaps. This does not touch dm-0092 (identitySeed is still correctly ignored for player) — that decision was about a DIFFERENT axis (per-instance identity) and stands unchanged.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (correctness bug, caught before any downstream slice built on the wrong assumption).
decisions.misconception_created: None — the fix is unconditional and the regression is now pinned by GameFeel.test.ts's anticipation-frame integration test.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: N/A — this was a straightforward bug fix once the failing integration test identified it; no alternative design was considered.

decisions.title: S9.6 audio cues are strictly category-bound (landed/defeat/goal only) — no generic jump-liftoff cue, and no checkpoint cue since the mechanic does not exist
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-071 fixes an audio signature per STRUCTURAL CATEGORY (the six PaperStylePack.AUDIO_PATCH entries) — it does not name a "player action" audio channel. Early P9 planning prose (execution_plan.md's S9.6 deliverable text) listed "jump/defeat/goal/checkpoint/surface class" as example transitions to derive cues from, written before S9.6 actually read the codebase. A zero-assumption grep at S9.6 confirms Jump Once has no checkpoint mechanic anywhere (its whole design is the one-jump lock + instant restart, dm-0018) — there is nothing to derive a checkpoint cue from.
decisions.problem_it_solves: deriveAudioCues (render/audio/CueDerivation.ts) emits exactly three cue shapes, each resolving to one of the six EXISTING category patches: landed (categorized by what was landed on — tile or entity, reusing render/feel/ImpulseEvents.landed rather than re-deriving the edge), defeat (always danger), goal (always interactive). A generic jump-liftoff sound was considered and left out: jump is a player action with no bound structural category, and inventing a seventh "always danger-adjacent" or ad hoc patch outside the six-category system would be exactly the kind of ungoverned signature REQ-070 exists to prevent. If a jump sound is wanted later, it needs its own explicitly-designed data channel (a genuine new scope decision), not a silent stretch of the category system.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (scope honesty, correcting pre-implementation planning prose against the real codebase).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session must not assume a checkpoint mechanic is coming later in P9/P10 just because early planning prose mentioned the word — it does not exist and was never designed; the one-jump-lock axiom is the entire save-state model.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Binding a jump cue to an arbitrary existing category (e.g., always "safe") was rejected: it would make that category's sound mean two unrelated things (its own structural signal AND every jump), diluting REQ-070's "no mixing signatures" intent even though jump itself is not a rendered category — the principle still applies to what a category's SOUND is understood to mean.

decisions.title: AudioExecutor.muteForBreak() stops scheduleCues from issuing ANY device calls while muted, not just silences them
decisions.why_it_exists: Poki's documented ad-break requirement (verified against sdk.poki.com at P9 planning, dm-0086) is "audio and keyboard input disabled during commercialBreaks". A literal reading could be satisfied by merely setting the master gain to 0 while still constructing and scheduling oscillator/gain nodes underneath (silent but resource-churning) — a shallower compliance than the spirit of "disabled".
decisions.problem_it_solves: createAudioExecutor tracks its own `muted` boolean independently of AudioDevice.setMasterGain (which it also calls, for defense-in-depth): scheduleCues checks `muted` FIRST and returns immediately, issuing zero device calls at all while muted — proven by a test asserting the device trace length is unchanged across a scheduleCues call made while muted. resume() restores both the gain and the gating.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (compliance correctness).
decisions.misconception_created: None — this is a straightforward strengthening of the mute contract beyond the minimum a literal reading would require.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Muting only via setMasterGain(0) (letting cues still schedule silently) was rejected as a shallower, resource-wasteful interpretation of "disabled" that a stricter, still-simple implementation avoids for free.

decisions.title: RenderIsolation.test.ts's fetch-confinement check refined to distinguish a real global fetch() call from an injected AssetFetcher seam's method
decisions.why_it_exists: S9.1's dm-0082 browser-global scan forbade any `fetch(` outside render/platform/ with a blanket regex. S9.7 introduced render/assets/AssetFetcher.ts, whose whole purpose is to DECLARE a `fetch(url: string): Promise<FetchOutcome>` method on an injected interface (never the browser global) and CALL it as `fetcher.fetch(url)` inside AssetLoader.ts — both legitimate, both flagged as false positives by the original blanket pattern.
decisions.problem_it_solves: The scan now special-cases fetch: it still forbids a bare, uncalled-on-an-object `fetch(...)` that is not followed by a `): ReturnType` signature annotation (the actual global-call shape this rule exists to catch), while explicitly allowing `.fetch(` (a method call on any injected instance) and `fetch(...): Promise<...>` (an interface/type member declaration). This is the same class of pattern every other device seam (Gl2Device, AudioDevice, Raster2D) already gets right by construction — fetch needed special-casing only because "fetch" is both a common, legitimate method name AND the specific browser global this rule polices, unlike e.g. `getContext(` which has no legitimate non-browser meaning in this codebase.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (test-infrastructure correctness).
decisions.misconception_created: None — the refined check is strictly narrower than before (fewer false positives), never broader; nothing that was correctly forbidden before is now allowed.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Renaming AssetFetcher's method away from `fetch` (e.g., to `deliver` or `request`) to dodge the scan was rejected: `fetch` is the correct, idiomatic name for what the seam does, and the scan should be precise rather than the code awkward.

decisions.title: RenderIsolation.test.ts's devicePixelRatio check removed as a redundant false-positive source — window is already forbidden separately
decisions.why_it_exists: S9.8's render/shell/ViewportModel.ts legitimately declares a `devicePixelRatio: number` field on its pure ViewportInput data type — the ALREADY-READ DPR value the caller injects, never a reference to the browser global. The blanket `/\bdevicePixelRatio\b/` scan pattern (S9.1) flagged this field declaration as if it were a live global access.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Removed the standalone devicePixelRatio pattern from the forbidden list: the real hazard it existed to catch — `window.devicePixelRatio` — is already caught by the separate, still-active `\bwindow\b` pattern, since `window` itself cannot appear outside render/platform/. A bare `devicePixelRatio` identifier is legitimate anywhere in render/ as a data field/parameter name; only combined with `window.` is it an actual global read, and that combination is still forbidden.
