Same class of fix as dm-0101's fetch( narrowing.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (test-infrastructure correctness).
decisions.misconception_created: None — strictly narrower than before; window.devicePixelRatio anywhere outside render/platform/ is still caught via the window check.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Renaming the field to something like dprHint to dodge the scan was rejected: devicePixelRatio is the correct, standard name for this value; the scan should be precise instead.

decisions.title: render/platform/ is deliberately NOT created in S9.8 — real browser-global bindings are deferred until a real browser/build pipeline exists (P11)
decisions.why_it_exists: S9.8's deliverable list (execution_plan.md) names both render/shell/*.ts and render/platform/*.ts. render/platform/ is defined (dm-0081/dm-0086) as the ONLY place real browser globals (canvas context creation, requestAnimationFrame, the PokiSDK CDN script global, fullscreen APIs, a real AudioContext) may be named — but this project has no build/bundle pipeline or browser test harness yet (P11 owns "automated build pipeline"; the P9 plan's own open question 3 already flagged "browser smoke harness timing" as unresolved, deferring real-browser verification to P11). Writing browser-binding code now would mean shipping logic that has literally never executed, guessed against documented APIs rather than proven against a real environment — exactly the kind of ungrounded work the zero-assumption and no-placeholder disciplines exist to prevent.
decisions.problem_it_solves: S9.8 delivers render/shell/{GameShell,ViewportModel,InputCapture,PortalSdk,PortalLifecycle}.ts complete and fully tested: every seam (Engine/StateManager/CANONICAL_PIPELINE reuse, compileScene, camera, audio cues, defeat marker, Poki lifecycle ordering) is real, working, verified logic. What is NOT built is the thin adapter layer that would bind these to an actual canvas/rAF/AudioContext/PokiSDK global — that binding is pure plumbing with no logic of its own once written, and it can only be honestly verified once a real browser environment exists to run it in (P11's build pipeline + browser smoke test, per the standing open question). No stub/placeholder files are created under render/platform/ — the directory does not exist yet, which is more honest than an empty or guessed-at stand-in.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (scope honesty under a real environment constraint).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session must not assume the game can run in an actual browser after S9.8 — every seam is proven against hand-rolled fakes only. render/platform/ remains the FIRST thing P11's build-pipeline work (or an earlier session that sets up a real browser harness) must create; RenderIsolation.test.ts's `if (file.startsWith('platform/')) continue;` clauses are already written and will apply the moment real files appear there.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Writing best-guess platform bindings now (a plausible-looking canvas/rAF/AudioContext adapter, untested) was rejected: it would read as "done" in the PKG/backlog while being unverified guesswork, and a subtle mismatch with the real API (a wrong event name, a wrong buffer layout) would surface only much later, in a context with less memory of why the code was written that way.

decisions.title: Debug overlays are semantic, device-free primitives (rect/polyline/label) — never forced through the BitmapHandle/DrawItem texture-atlas pipeline
decisions.why_it_exists: S9.9's render/tooling/Overlay.ts paints the six P8 debug descriptor kinds (hitbox AABBs, trigger wiring lines, mover paths, the jump-arc preview, the ground normal, the physics-state snapshot). None of these are gameplay-grammar visuals — they have no StylePack role, no palette accent, no cached bitmap. Coercing them through StylePack.visual()/rasterize() would mean inventing a fake VisualRequest role for pure debug wireframes, polluting the one seam (dm-0083) that exists to keep art style swappable.
decisions.problem_it_solves: paintOverlays returns an OverlayDrawList of rect/polyline/label primitives tagged with a semantic `source` (hitbox/trigger/path/jumpArc/normal/physicsState) and no colour. Exactly like render/shell's deferred render/platform/ (dm-0103), the actual stroke-colour-per-source mapping is a future executor's job once a real canvas exists — this stays pure data, testable without any device.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (architectural purity of the StylePack seam).
decisions.misconception_created: A future slice must not assume render/tooling/Overlay.ts produces anything drawable on its own — it is one more device-free layer, same as every other render/ subtree pre-render/platform/.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Giving each primitive a hardcoded hex colour was rejected: colour-as-a-literal-in-logic is exactly the anti-pattern the whole StylePack seam exists to prevent, even for debug-only chrome — and it buys nothing since no executor exists yet to consume it.

decisions.title: render/scene/SceneCompiler.ts's terrain-autotile helpers (isSolidTile/terrainMask/terrainVariant) narrowed from WorldState to bare TilemapDef, and exported alongside visualItem — reused verbatim by S9.9's EditorSurface
decisions.why_it_exists: S9.9's EditorSurface.compileEditorScene needs to paint a P8 LevelDraft's terrain with the exact same autotile mask/variant the shipped game uses (WYSIWYG editing) — but a LevelDraft has no WorldState, only a bare TilemapDef (structurally identical to WorldState.level.tilemap). The three helpers only ever touched world.level.tilemap internally.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Narrowing their parameter from WorldState to TilemapDef is a pure signature change (S9.3 is COMPLETED-not-VERIFIED, so amendment is allowed per the four-state discipline, precedented twice already this phase: dm-0090, dm-0095) — compileScene's own call sites were updated to pass world.level.tilemap, behavior unchanged, all S9.3/S9.4 tests still green. This lets EditorSurface reuse the identical terrain-projection logic with ZERO duplication, instead of hand-rolling a second autotile mask calculation that could silently drift from the shipped renderer's.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (duplication-avoidance, correctness-by-construction).
decisions.misconception_created: None — the exported helpers are strictly more general (any TilemapDef-shaped caller) while compileScene's own behavior is provably unchanged (same tests, same call pattern with one extra field access).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Re-deriving the mask/variant math independently inside EditorSurface was rejected outright: EditorState.ts's own header already documents "no duplicated shape exists" as a load-bearing LevelDraft design principle, and two renderers computing terrain autotile independently is precisely the kind of drift the project's single-source-of-truth discipline forbids.

decisions.title: InspectorBindings.applyInspectorCommand is a closed UI-command union dispatching to exactly one Inspector method call each — no new logic
decisions.why_it_exists: The execution plan's S9.9 deliverable asks for "inspector bindings expose pause/step/reload/variable-edit as UI commands calling P8's Inspector verbatim." dm-0070 already settled how setVariable must work (commit through StateManager, never in-place) entirely inside tools/debug/Inspector.ts.
decisions.problem_it_solves: A UI panel needs a single dispatchable command type (e.g. for a keybinding table or button row) rather than calling five differently-shaped Inspector methods directly. applyInspectorCommand is a pure switch with one line per case — it cannot drift from Inspector's own behavior because it does nothing but call it.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: None.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Adding batching, debouncing, or a command-history log at this layer was rejected as scope creep beyond what S9.9 asks for — undo/redo already exists at the EDITOR-draft layer (EditorState.ts), and the Inspector's pause/step/reload/setVariable controls have no analogous history requirement in the P8 design.

decisions.title: Quality tiers gate a generic nonCriticalBudget truncation over DrawList, not a per-item particle/decoration/grain classification
decisions.why_it_exists: The original P9 planning prose (design summary point 12, written before S9.2-S9.4 landed) described the tier ladder as "full -> reduced-particles -> no-deferred-decoration -> no-grain," implying per-category dropping. Zero-assumption check at S9.10 start found this does not correspond to anything in the shipped DrawItem shape: particles are spawned directly into render/pool/ParticlePool.ts and never enter a DrawList at all (render/feel/ImpulseEvents.spawnBurst acquires pool handles, full stop); grain and decor are baked INSIDE PaperStylePack.rasterize()'s bitmap content (render/style/paper/Geometry.ts's grain(), the 8-type decor set) — neither is a separate, taggable DrawItem field. DrawItem.critical (pinned since S9.3/S9.4 with a comment literally naming "the quality controller (S9.10)") is the ONLY quality-relevant field that actually exists.
decisions.problem_it_solves: QualityTier.applyTier(drawList, tier) keeps every critical:true item unconditionally and truncates critical:false items to tier.nonCriticalBudget, order-preserving. This is a faithful, honest realization of the REQ-016 x REQ-163 interlock against the CODE AS IT ACTUALLY SHIPPED, not a re-derivation of a decor/particle taxonomy that would require inventing new DrawItem fields (and duplicating classification logic already implicit in how each render/ subtree already decided what is/isn't critical, via render/grammar/Grammar.ts's category.critical and render/feel/DefeatMarker.ts's always-critical marker).
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (zero-assumption correction of stale planning prose against actual shipped code).
decisions.misconception_created: A future slice must not assume DrawItem carries a decor/particle/grain kind tag — it does not. If P11 or a later phase wants tier-differentiated dropping (e.g. drop grain before decor before entities), that requires a NEW DrawItem field written by the SceneCompiler itself (the same pattern dm-0093 already established for "pressurePlate/spring pressed state would need a new WorldState field, never re-derived logic").
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Re-deriving a decor/particle/grain classification inside render/quality/ by pattern-matching bitmap ids or VisualRequest.role strings was rejected: it would silently duplicate categorization logic that belongs to whichever module actually produces each DrawItem, and would drift the moment a StylePack or the scene compiler changed its role/state naming.

decisions.title: A new top-level content/ subtree, one-way-isolated, holds P10's authored/generated data payloads and their schema+assembler logic
decisions.why_it_exists: docs/directory_structure.md names no home for persisted level/chapter/campaign data: src/gen/ is design-time LOGIC (dm-0057's own isolation rule), src/eval/campaign/ holds runtime RECORD types that consume data rather than own it, and src/schema/ holds wire-format types with no content of their own. P10 is the first phase to persist real gameplay data into the repo.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Gives authored content a home that mirrors the project's existing isolation discipline (src/gen/, tools/, render/ each got the same treatment at their phase's start) instead of overloading an existing subtree's meaning. content/ may import src/gen/ and src/eval/ only through named public seams; nothing under src/ may ever import content/.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (structural/architectural decision).
decisions.misconception_created: None — content/ is purely additive; no existing subtree's contract changes.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Putting JSON payloads inside src/gen/ conflates generation logic with generation output and gives the GenIsolation scan an unintended new file class to reason about. Putting them in src/eval/campaign/ couples authored data to the runtime-record shapes that merely consume it. Reusing src/schema/ wholesale ignores that every prior versioned-profile schema (ScoringProfile, CampaignProfile, GenProfile, TelemetryProfile) already lives beside its consuming subtree, not centralized.

decisions.title: content/schema/ChapterFramework.ts is a new strict-parsed schema for REQ-083's seven-step framework, and 'before any level generation' is enforced structurally, not by convention
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-083 requires Theme/Learning Goal/Mental Model/Misconceptions/Subversion/Optimization/Final Exam be defined before any level generation, but nothing in the codebase enforced 'all seven present' or 'before' as anything other than prose.
decisions.problem_it_solves: parseChapterFramework rejects incomplete frameworks (empty textual fields, zero misconceptions, duplicate slot ids, out-of-set archetype/arcPhase values). content/Assembler.ts's buildConceptFromSlot is the only code path that constructs a LevelConcept for manufactureLevel, and it only ever takes an already-parsed ChapterFramework slot as input — there is no way to generate a level without a validated framework upstream, making the ordering a structural proof instead of a promise.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: A future slice must not add any second code path that builds a LevelConcept without going through an already-parsed ChapterFramework slot — that would silently reopen the ordering gap this decision closes.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Leaving REQ-083 as a documentation-only convention was rejected as exactly the kind of unenforced ordering rule this project's structural-scan culture (GenIsolation, ToolsIsolation, RenderIsolation) exists to avoid.

decisions.title: REQ-100 Dual-Path is proven by composition over the existing OptimizationVerdict, not by a new path-counting mechanism or literal alternate geometry
decisions.why_it_exists: Nothing in src/ names discovery/mastery paths, and a naive reading of REQ-100 risks inventing a whole new simulation-side mechanism. src/eval/local/Optimization.ts's OptimizationVerdict already computes applicable (a discovery completion and a search/archetype-derived optimum both exist) and deltaSeconds (Time_Discovery minus Time_WorldRecord, REQ-102's own metric) — exactly the evidence dual-path-ness requires.
decisions.problem_it_solves: content/DualPathProof.ts's proveDualPath(verdict, profile) is a pure wrapper: applicable === true AND deltaSeconds >= profile.dualPath.minDeltaSeconds. For a one-jump precision platformer, the 'two paths in one physical space' REQ-100 demands are execution-quality routes through identical geometry, not branching level layout — which is what this composition actually measures.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: A future slice must not assume REQ-100 requires authored alternate physical routes or branching geometry — it does not, and adding such would violate REQ-005.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Authoring literal alternate physical routes per level was rejected: REQ-005 explicitly rejects depth via expanded power sets, and duplicate geometry undermines the one-jump constraint's singularity. Inventing a new simulation-side path-counting mechanism was rejected as a repeat of the dm-0107 lesson — never re-derive classification logic a lower layer (Optimization.ts) already computed.

decisions.title: REQ-084 difficulty tiers are derived from measured evidence (content/DifficultyEstimator.ts), never substituted from the authored difficultyTarget intent vector
decisions.why_it_exists: Three unrelated 'tier'/'difficulty' concepts already coexist in the codebase: TierTimes (Optimization.ts's routing tiers — discovery/good/fast/expert/worldRecord, a per-level time-based skill anchor set, REQ-101), DifficultyAxis/difficultyVectors (an authored [0,1] intent vector on LevelConcept, marked provisional since dm-0015 and never validated against outcomes), and CurriculumLevel.difficulty (an unspecified scalar the caller must supply). None of these is the REQ-084 campaign-bucket (Easy/Medium/Hard/Harder/Very-Hard) a level belongs to, and conflating any of them with it was a real risk.
decisions.problem_it_solves: estimateDifficultyTier(evidence, verdict, profile) is a calibrated, weighted function of measured TierTimes.discovery, agent attempt/failure counts from the EvidenceBundle, and deltaSeconds — never of difficultyTarget, which remains only the generator's aim point (Generator.ts's existing, unchanged usage). Weights and bucket boundaries live in a versioned DifficultyProfile, satisfying REQ-084's own 'calibrated data, not hardcoded' wording.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: A future slice must not read LevelConcept.difficultyTarget as a level's shipped difficulty tier — it is only the authored aim point the generator targets, not the measured outcome.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Using difficultyTarget directly as the shipped tier was rejected as the easy-but-wrong path: it validates nothing, since it is exactly the value the generator was told to hit, not what the level was measured to actually demand of a player. Using TierTimes' routing tiers directly was rejected because they answer a different question (skill-routing within one level, not a level's place in a five-bucket campaign curriculum).

decisions.title: content/Assembler.ts::integrateProduct is the literal, previously-unbuilt REQ-090 phase 8 (Campaign Integration) — new P10 code, not a P7 retrofit
decisions.why_it_exists: PipelinePhase (src/gen/Pipeline.ts) has seven members, not eight: 'Targeted Revision' correctly folds into the evolutionary loop between phases 3/4, but 'Campaign Integration' (REQ-090's prose phase 8) corresponds to no code — manufactureLevel's accept branch returns a PipelineProduct and stops. Assuming this phase already wrote into a campaign structure would have been an unread-file guess.
decisions.problem_it_solves: integrateProduct(product, chapterId, macroVerdict): LevelRecord derives every LevelRecord field directly from the PipelineProduct's own fields (zero re-computation) and stamps the chapter's MacroVerdict onto it. This closes the actual gap between 'a level was manufactured' and 'a level is part of a validated campaign.'
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: A future slice must not assume P7 already delivered campaign integration — P7 closed VERIFIED on exactly the seven phases it owns (dm-0064); phase 8 is P10's, because it is only meaningful once real chapters exist to integrate into.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Re-opening src/gen/Pipeline.ts to add an eighth PipelinePhase enum value was rejected: P7 is VERIFIED and dm-0064 already closed its scope; adding campaign-shaped logic to src/gen/ would also violate its own isolation rule (dm-0057: gen/ never reads campaign/ as more than types).

decisions.title: Full-campaign GDOS/macro-curriculum evaluation runs via a separate npm run validate:campaign script, not inside npm test
decisions.why_it_exists: evaluateLevel runs 5 archetypes x 4 local audits per level; running this across a real campaign's levels plus processCampaign across all chapters inside the existing ~5-second, 711-test suite would make suite runtime grow with content volume instead of code volume, re-running full campaign evaluation on every unrelated code change through P10's batch slices (S10.4) and beyond.
decisions.problem_it_solves: tools/sdlc/validateCampaign.js (dm-0113) calls the same content/ assembler + evaluateLevel/processCampaign functions already unit-tested in isolation, and writes a committed, schema-versioned content/data/validation-report.json that S10.5/S10.6 read as the source of truth for the phase exit condition. npm test keeps one content-volume-independent smoke chapter (the S10.3 pilot) plus schema/estimator unit tests, so its runtime stays flat as chapters are added.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: A future slice must not add new levels' evaluation as individual npm test cases — that would silently regress suite runtime growth back to content-volume-coupled, undoing this decision.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Keeping full-campaign validation inside npm test was rejected as the default because it directly contradicts the pattern established at every prior phase (a flat, fast, code-coupled suite) and would penalize every future session's fast-feedback loop for a content-volume reason unrelated to code correctness.

decisions.title: Content generation uses overgenerate-and-curate (Creativity.evolveLevel's existing REQ-081 loop selects the highest-scoring evaluateLevel candidate per slot), not primary hand-authoring via the editor
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-050 requires all creative decisions originate from GDOS, with no gameplay content created independently of it. Three generation modes were weighed: fully unattended procedural generation, hand-authoring via the P8/P9 editor then validating after the fact, and overgenerate-and-curate.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Under overgenerate-and-curate, several candidates per slot are generated via evolveLevel, all scored via evaluateLevel, and the highest-scoring accepted candidate is selected by code in content/Assembler.ts, with the selection ledgered. This makes REQ-050 true by construction rather than by after-the-fact review. The P8/P9 editor remains available only as a touch-up path for a near-miss candidate, and any touch-up re-enters evaluateLevel before acceptance — it never bypasses scoring. Every accepted level's generation seed (already present in PipelineProduct.provenance.seed) is persisted into its content/data/ payload, so campaign regeneration from the same framework + seed set is byte-identical and idempotent.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: A future slice must not treat the editor as the PRIMARY authoring path for campaign levels — it is a touch-up path only, and any output from it must still pass evaluateLevel before it can be accepted.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Hand-authoring via the editor as the primary path was rejected: an editor-authored level's creative content would originate from a human/session's taste with GDOS only checking it after the fact, which is close to unsatisfiable under REQ-050's own wording. Fully unattended single-candidate generation was rejected as leaving no mechanism to prefer a better-scoring alternative when one exists, wasting the P7 creativity loop and P5 CDRE machinery that were built for exactly this kind of selection.

decisions.title: P10 empirical finding: the P7 single gap-corridor generator cannot produce content passing DEFAULT_PROFILE (surprise structurally ~0; four approach-modifying mechanics softlock the one-jump corridor) — honest-close path chosen (user-approved)
decisions.why_it_exists: A 144-level sweep through the real pipeline (S10.3 start, zero-assumption) established that under DEFAULT_PROFILE every generated gap-corridor passes info-density but FAILS the emotional gate (surprise 0/95 on ALL 144 levels — surprise = 32 x deaths-before-a-clear, and the deterministic archetype agents almost always either clean-clear or time out, so plan-invalidation is never registered) and streamability. A second probe found the four approach-modifying mechanics (movingPlatform, collapsingFloor, iceSurface, conveyor) softlock or make unsolvable ~100% of one-jump corridors (a spent jump plus a modified approach traps the player), while seven mechanic configs (spike-pit, laser, movingHazard, spring, gravityZone, pressurePlate+door, proximityZone) are clean. This invalidated the P10 plan's central premise that 'every mechanism is built; P10 is just wiring + data'.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Surfaced the fork honestly to the user, who chose 'Machinery + honest close': build and test ALL content/ machinery, calibrate a content ScoringProfile via CDRE from the achievable distribution, generate the real campaign through the actual pipeline, flip only genuinely-satisfied REQs to VERIFIED, and keep REQ-005/REQ-015 IN_PROGRESS with an explicit note that genuine surprise/betrayal (Level-Devil-style subversion) requires generator template enrichment. No gate pass is faked; VERIFIED P7 is not reopened.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (zero-assumption feasibility finding that reshaped the phase).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session must not read the shipped P10 campaign as evidence that the generator produces rich, surprising content — it produces one-jump gap-corridors with clean mechanics only; genuine surprise, difficulty spread, and the four approach-modifying mechanics need a richer generator template library (a scoped P7+ follow-on), which is why REQ-005/REQ-012/REQ-015/REQ-084's content-satisfaction remain IN_PROGRESS.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Enriching the generator (new subversion templates in src/gen/) was weighed and set aside for this session: it reopens VERIFIED P7, is multi-session R&D with uncertain payoff (the deterministic agents may still under-register 'surprise'), and the user did not select it. Calibrate-and-declare-done was rejected as dishonest (it lowers gates to ship structurally-similar low-surprise levels and flips the full REQ set to VERIFIED without caveats — the DoD's forbidden anti-pattern).

decisions.title: content-calibration-v1: a CDRE recalibration of the ScoringProfile thresholds (only) to the single-template generator's achievable distribution; campaign built on the clean-mechanic palette
decisions.why_it_exists: DEFAULT_PROFILE's emotional (surprise/mastery 95) and streamability (85-95) thresholds were set at P5 as the PRD's aspirational numbers and were never calibrated against real generated content (dm-0031); the P7 single-template generator cannot reach them (dm-0115). To generate any real campaign, the profile's thresholds must match what the generator's best candidates actually achieve.
decisions.problem_it_solves: content/data/calibration-profile.json is DEFAULT_PROFILE with ONLY its gate thresholds lowered to the achievable band (emotional curiosity/confidence 35, surprise 0, mastery 3; streamability shareability 5, clip 0, reaction 5, replay 5); every estimator COEFFICIENT stays at the DEFAULT baseline, so the recalibration is a pure threshold move a future richer generator can raise back. Under it, the clean-mechanic palette produces pipeline-accepted levels at 14-24/24 seed-spread accept rates, and overgenerate-and-curate (dm-0114) still discriminates by total gate score. This is CDRE (the P5 profile-evolution channel), ledgered, distribution-level — NOT a per-level threshold override to ship a specific weak level (the DoD's forbidden case).
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: A future session must not treat content-calibration-v1's surprise=0 / low streamability thresholds as the game's quality bar — they encode the current generator's ceiling, not a design target. A richer generator should raise them back toward DEFAULT and re-verify. The surprise gate in particular is effectively ungated under this profile (a documented generator limitation, not a design choice).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Moving estimator coefficients (not just thresholds) was rejected as it would distort the scores themselves rather than the pass line, making cross-profile comparison meaningless. Keeping DEFAULT_PROFILE and generating nothing was rejected as failing to deliver any P10 content. Calibrating the manifest's REQ-084 target distribution to the estimator's actual output (rather than the design-intent 20/35/25/15/5) was rejected as circular gaming — the target stays the design intent and the measured gap is reported honestly.

decisions.title: content/Assembler.ts maps CurriculumLevel.hasExploit to false for pipeline-accepted products — the entity-exploit proxy's one-jump-pit misfire (dm-0041) must not fail macro health
decisions.why_it_exists: The macro curriculum validation's checkHealth requires every level be solvable, softlock-free, AND exploit-free. But the entity-exploit proxy provably MISFIRES on a well-formed one-jump pit (dm-0041/dm-0064): it flags 'jumping over the pit' — the intended one-jump solution — as a skip that avoids the hazard envelope. The P7 pipeline itself deliberately does NOT gate on this proxy for exactly this reason, relying on jump-necessity as the load-bearing challenge check.
decisions.problem_it_solves: assembleChapterRecord only ever consumes pipeline-ACCEPTED products, which the pipeline proves jump-necessary. So toCurriculumLevel sets hasExploit=false: the exploit-proxy flag on these levels is the known pit-misfire, and re-counting it at the macro-health layer would make EVERY corridor-based chapter fail macro validation on a signal the pipeline already, correctly, ignores. solvable and hasSoftlock are still propagated honestly from the evidence (both are real pipeline gates).
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A.
decisions.misconception_created: A future session must not read hasExploit=false in the assembler as 'exploits are never checked' — it is specifically the one-jump-pit misfire being excluded for pipeline-accepted (jump-necessary) products. If a future generator produces levels with REAL skip exploits that also pass jump-necessity, this mapping must be revisited (distinguish the pit-spike misfire from a genuine bypass via the bypassedHazardIds set).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Propagating ev.exploit.hasExploit verbatim was rejected because it makes every one-jump-pit chapter fail macro health on a documented false positive, contradicting the pipeline's own deliberate non-gating (dm-0041/dm-0064). Adding a real exploit-vs-misfire discriminator to the assembler was deferred as unnecessary while the only generator template IS the one-jump pit — there is no genuine-bypass case to distinguish yet.

decisions.title: P11 enriches the generator with subversion templates so REQ-005/084/015 reach genuine VERIFIED, rather than relaxing the IRD every-REQ-VERIFIED exit gate (user-decided)
decisions.why_it_exists: P10 shipped REQ-005 (Level-Devil subversion), REQ-084 (five-tier difficulty spread) and REQ-015 (emotional-arc delivery) IN_PROGRESS because the single gap-corridor generator scores surprise ~0 and bins bimodally (dm-0115). REQ-173 demands definitive premium-indie quality (complete only when all systems shipped-verified) and the IRD P11 exit gate says every REQ is VERIFIED. Ship-and-disclose would force formally relaxing that gate.
decisions.problem_it_solves: New subversion candidate templates are added to the src/gen/ parametric generator (telegraph-then-betray geometry, fake/dissolving floors, delayed/triggered hazards) so surprise (deaths-before-clear vs the deterministic archetypes) becomes genuinely nonzero and the difficulty estimator can span all five tiers; content-calibration-v1 thresholds are raised back toward DEFAULT_PROFILE (content-calibration-v2, ledgered CDRE) and the campaign is regenerated and revalidated. Guardrails: manufactureLevel public seam signature stays byte-stable; every new template output passes the UNCHANGED Solvability/Softlock/evaluateLevel gates (the dm-0115 softlock failure mode is provably avoided, not hoped); all P7 tests re-run green -- a VERIFIED subtree extended additively under P11 ownership of REQ-005, never structurally reopened (dm-0064 seven-phase pipeline closure untouched).
decisions.emotion_targeted: Surprise/Betrayal (REQ-015 phase 3) -- the arc phase the gap-corridor generator could structurally place but not emotionally deliver.
decisions.misconception_created: A future session might read P11-modifies-src/gen/ as license to reopen the P7 pipeline structure. It is NOT: only the template PALETTE is extended additively behind the frozen manufactureLevel seam; the seven PipelinePhase members and the pipeline control flow are closed and unchanged.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Ship-and-disclose (keep REQ-005/084/015 IN_PROGRESS, relax the IRD exit gate to VERIFIED-or-honestly-disclosed-generator-limited) was the smaller path but ships M6 with a known content ceiling and fails REQ-173 completion bar literally. Decide-at-audit (defer the fork to S11.5) risks a late scope surprise after build+platform are sunk. The user chose enrichment.

decisions.title: REQ-160 is an allocation-BOUNDED audit, not literal zero-allocation -- the immutable copy-on-write core stays; zero-alloc discipline applies only to the render hot path
decisions.why_it_exists: Engine.tick allocates a fresh spread snapshot per fixed step and every System.step returns a new spread state; StateManager retains current+previous. That copy-on-write IS the determinism/immutability correctness contract (dm-0004), which explicitly deferred allocation optimization to P11 and scoped it to pooled entities (particles), not the top-level tree. A literal zero-allocation sim rewrite would destroy immutability and break the replay determinism the validation agents depend on -- a single-source-of-truth invariant violation.
decisions.problem_it_solves: S11.1 deliverable is a Profiler-measured PROOF that (a) the sim allocates exactly one snapshot per fixed step -- bounded, predictable, GC-friendly, NOT zero, a deliberate documented correctness tradeoff -- and (b) the render hot path (compileScene, ParticlePool, the GL batcher) is allocation-free in steady state via the pools P9 already built (REQ-161/162). Any steady-state allocation in the render path outside the pools is the actual bug the audit hunts; the sim tree is out of scope by design.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (performance/architectural decision).
decisions.misconception_created: A future reader of REQ-160 text (zero-allocation runtime loop) might think the sim loop failed the requirement. It did not: bounded is the correct, deliberate reading given dm-0004; the zero-alloc intent is satisfied where achievable and correct (the render pools) and consciously traded for correctness where it is not (the immutable sim tree).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Literal zero-alloc sim (mutable pre-allocated state buffers, in-place system updates) rejected: destroys immutability, determinism, replay-tape validity, and the single mutation-point invariant -- the entire correctness foundation P1 established. Pooling the top-level snapshot tree rejected: its shape varies with entity count and pooling it reintroduces the aliasing bugs immutability was adopted to prevent.

decisions.title: The REQ-172 build pipeline is a zero-dependency hand-rolled bundler in tools/build/ over tsc dist/ output; the Poki SDK is CDN-referenced not bundled; asset optimization means JS+JSON minify only (user-decided)
decisions.why_it_exists: package.json has tsc->dist/ ESM and node --test, no bundler, no index.html, no packaging; devDeps limited to typescript+@types/node. A Poki game ships as a browser-loadable bundle, but .npmrc pins the public registry because the private mirror is broken, and the standing policy is zero new npm dependencies. PortalSdk.ts documents that Poki server-pushes SDK updates from its CDN and bundling is not the supported path.
decisions.problem_it_solves: tools/build/ (bundle.js: zero-dep ESM-graph resolver+concatenator+minifier; emitHtml.js: generates index.html script-tagging the Poki CDN SDK + the bundled client; optimizeData.js: JSON-minifies content/data/) wired to npm run build:release -> dist/release/. Because the art is procedurally rasterized at runtime and cached by key (dm-0080), there are essentially no static image assets, so the REQ-172 asset optimization pass concretely means minifying the client JS and the content/data JSON payloads -- not an image pipeline (stated so the slice is not padded with a stage that has no inputs).
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (build/infrastructure decision).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session might try to add esbuild/vite for a real build. The mirror is still broken and the zero-dep policy still holds; the hand-rolled bundler is the sanctioned path. Also: the Poki SDK must NEVER be bundled -- index.html references it from the CDN.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: esbuild/vite rejected: one-or-more new deps, install may fail on the broken mirror, breaks the zero-new-deps policy. Native-ESM + import-map with no bundle rejected: dozens of module HTTP requests directly harm REQ-001 first-30-seconds retention and REQ-002 no-long-loads. Bundling the Poki SDK rejected: breaks Poki documented server-push update model (PortalSdk.ts).

decisions.title: S11.2 builds render/platform/ as the sole browser-global-naming subtree (resolving dm-0103 deferral), wires the real Poki rewardedBreak, and is verified by a documented manual browser-open checkpoint -- not a Playwright dependency
decisions.why_it_exists: render/platform/ does not exist -- dm-0103 deferred the real browser-global bindings to P11. It is the only subtree permitted to name browser globals: Canvas2D/WebGL2 context creation, the requestAnimationFrame FrameScheduler, the fetch AssetFetcher, fullscreen, and the real PokiSDK script-tag binding the PortalSdk seam NullPortalSdk currently doubles for. Every render module below it is already pure logic against injected hexagonal device interfaces (dm-0086), so render/platform/ is thin, logic-free construction glue -- but it is the first time the game runs in a real browser, and it cannot run in node --test.
decisions.problem_it_solves: S11.2 builds the thin binding modules (Canvas, RafScheduler, HttpAssetFetcher, PokiPortalSdk, Fullscreen) + a Main.ts client entry, wires real rewardedBreak through the shell (NullPortalSdk still the headless test double so the wiring stays fake-testable except the thin real binding), and adds the sixth subtree-scoped isolation scan: no file outside render/platform/ names a browser global, and render/platform/ files contain construction only (no branching/state). Verification is a documented, recorded, repeatable manual browser-open checkpoint in the S11.7 report (render a level, confirm animation/audio-cues, confirm rewardedBreak, confirm fullscreen+responsive resize) -- honest because every module below is fake-proven and the platform files have no logic to unit-test.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (platform/infrastructure decision).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session might treat the manual checkpoint as untested-equals-unverified. The coverage is explicitly disclosed, not assumed: the logic is fake-proven headlessly; only the logic-free construction glue relies on the manual open. Automating it (Playwright-class) is out of scope under the zero-dep policy unless that constraint is lifted.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: A Playwright-class automated smoke harness (P9 open question 3) rejected: heavy dependency + browser-binary download, same mirror/zero-dep collision as dm-0120, and the project has never run a real browser in its test loop. Putting browser globals in multiple render modules rejected: destroys the hexagonal-seam testability the whole render/ subtree was built on (dm-0086).

decisions.title: REQ-P07 (full 67-REQ PRD compliance audit) gets its own standalone slice (S11.5), re-checking every prior VERIFIED flip against SHIPPED evidence, not prior reports claims
decisions.why_it_exists: REQ-P07 (perform a full PRD compliance audit at every milestone) has been NOT_STARTED the entire project despite being chartered per-milestone. The naive stub folded it into one line with REQ-173. Every prior phase flipped REQs to VERIFIED against its OWN exit report; a real audit may surface a VERIFIED flip with a hidden P11 share, a double-count, or a requirement satisfied only for synthetic fixtures.
decisions.problem_it_solves: S11.5 is a standalone full-backlog sweep producing docs/verification/compliance_audit_M6.md -- every one of the 67 REQs re-checked against shipped evidence, every prior VERIFIED flip re-confirmed or corrected, every remaining IN_PROGRESS resolved to VERIFIED or an explicitly-accepted, ledgered non-goal -- run BEFORE any sign-off (S11.6/S11.7).
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (governance/audit decision).
decisions.misconception_created: None -- additive discipline. The prior per-phase verification reports remain valid as phase evidence; S11.5 is the final cross-cutting reconciliation, not a repudiation of them.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Folding REQ-P07 into the REQ-173 sign-off (the stub shape) rejected: a 67-REQ sweep is real work with real findings and must not be a rubber-stamp line item. Trusting prior reports VERIFIED claims without re-checking shipped evidence rejected: defeats the entire purpose of a compliance audit.

decisions.title: The P11 slice table is restructured from the naive four-row stub to seven slices (S11.1-S11.7)
decisions.why_it_exists: The stub (S11.1 zero-alloc / S11.2 build / S11.3 audit / S11.4 sign-off) was authored at P8 close before any runtime/render/build substrate was read. The adversarial review found: S11.1 framed REQ-160 impossibly (dm-0119); no slice existed for the deferred render/platform/ bindings (dm-0121), the real Poki SDK runtime wiring (REQ-174), the generator-enrichment fork (dm-0118), or a standalone REQ-P07 audit (dm-0122); and REQ-161/162/163/132 release shares were invisibly folded into zero-alloc.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Seven slices: S11.1 allocation-bounded audit; S11.2 render/platform/ + real SDK + rewardedBreak + rAF conformance; S11.3 zero-dep build pipeline; S11.4 generator enrichment + campaign regeneration; S11.5 full 67-REQ compliance audit; S11.6 final subtractive pass + completion bar; S11.7 P11 verification report + M6 closure + sign-off. Sequencing puts the highest-risk slice (S11.4 enrichment) after the build machinery so a threshold miss cannot block shipping the machinery.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (planning decision).
decisions.misconception_created: None -- the restructure is documented in both docs/task_slices.md (Phase 11 table note) and docs/execution_plan.md (P11 section).
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Keeping the four-row stub rejected: it mapped four owned/shared REQ clusters to no slice and contained one literally-impossible slice. A single mega-slice rejected: mirrors P10 finding 5 error (sizing generate-the-whole-thing as one session) -- each area (audit, platform, build, enrichment, compliance) is independently session-sized.

decisions.title: Production real-time handling is already safe -- Clock.advance clamps to MAX_FRAME_SECONDS; P11 only obligation is an rAF-binding conformance test, not new spiral-of-death code
decisions.why_it_exists: The whole sim advances on realDeltaSeconds fed to Clock.advance. In production, rAF delivers variable wall-clock deltas that spike after a tab-switch/GC pause; a naive binding could bank hundreds of catch-up steps and hang a frame (Engine.tick loops for i<steps). This production failure mode was never exercised by the headless tests, which feed controlled deltas.
decisions.problem_it_solves: Direct read of Clock.ts confirms advance() already clamps realDeltaSeconds to MAX_FRAME_SECONDS = 0.25 (<=15 catch-up steps/frame) before banking -- the spiral-of-death is handled in the pure core. So the S11.2 obligation is only a binding-level conformance test that render/platform/RafScheduler feeds TIMESTAMP DELTAS (successive rAF timestamps subtracted), not an accumulated or re-derived clock, honoring the existing contract. No new protection code is written.
decisions.emotion_targeted: N/A (correctness/robustness decision).
decisions.misconception_created: A future session might add a second delta-clamp in the rAF binding, duplicating the Clock guarantee with a second source of truth. Do not: the clamp lives in Clock.advance; the binding only supplies an honest raw delta.
decisions.why_alternatives_rejected: Adding spiral-of-death protection in render/platform/ rejected: duplicates an existing, tested core guarantee (two sources of truth for one invariant). Feeding an accumulated clock from the binding rejected: bypasses the Clock own accumulator and breaks the determinism contract.

mechanic_lifecycle_registry.notes: Populated from Phase 3 onward. Tracks each mechanic across the 9-stage spectrum (Introduction→Reinforcement→Expansion→Combination→Subversion→Mastery→Dormancy→Reintroduction→Retirement).
